title: Are your Airbnb bookings down?
description: Are Your Airbnb Bookings Down? Let’s Talk Data, Strategy & Staying Competitive If you’ve found yourself staring at your Airbnb calendar lately wondering, “Where did the bookings go?” — you’re not alone. On this Hosting Hotline episode, a host named Courtney called in with a concern we hear often: fewer bookings and more cancellations. But is it just her? Or is something shifting across the short-term rental landscape? Let’s dig into the data, address the fears, and arm you with practical strategies to not only survive but thrive as a host. First, Take a Breath — Then Ask Better Questions When it comes to STR success, data is your best friend. While it’s easy to get swept up in the headlines or social chatter, your best insight comes from two things: Your own numbers — year over year, month over month. Trusted tools that analyze your market. Feelings can be loud. But facts — like pacing reports, booking windows, and comp sets — are what help you take action instead of panicking. Noticing Fewer Bookings? Ask Yourself: Are you comparing your current performance to your own past data? Have you looked at your market pacing ? Are you behind or on par? Are you using dynamic pricing software like PriceLabs ? Have you checked your availability settings or minimum night requirements? Have you reviewed your listing photos and descriptions with fresh eyes? Are you following up with previous guests to invite them back? title: The Spring Maintenance Checklist for Hosts
description: Spring Maintenance Checklist for Your Airbnb or Short-Term Rental As temperatures rise and peak season approaches, now is the time to refresh your short-term rental. Prevent emergency repairs, avoid guest complaints, and extend the life of your property with a strategic spring maintenance plan. Whether you're a hands-on host or managing a team, this checklist will help you prioritize the most important seasonal tasks—so your property looks great, functions smoothly, and delivers the 5-star guest experience you're known for. 1. Window Functionality & Cleanliness Open all windows and let the fresh air in—this also gives you the chance to spot any that don’t lock or open properly. Clean out the window tracks with a steamer or small brush. Reinstall clean, dust-free screens. Don’t forget to wash the outside of the windows too. First impressions matter. 2. HVAC Maintenance (Don’t Wait!) Schedule routine HVAC service before your first summer guest checks in. Replace or clean filters. Clear debris from outdoor units and clean coils if needed. For mini-splits, check all interior components—professional help is worth it if you’re unsure. 3. Restock Your “Forgot Something” Basket In addition to basic toiletries, add warm-weather extras like: Sunscreen Bug spray Aloe vera Mini fans or cooling towels (if appropriate) A thoughtful, seasonal amenity basket is one of the easiest ways to impress guests—and rack up 5-star reviews. 4. Patio & Outdoor Furniture Safety Check Check for rust, wear

title: Should I Bake My Airbnb Cleaning Fee Into the Nightly Rate?
description: Should You Bake the Cleaning Fee Into Your Airbnb Rate? If you’ve been hosting for a while, you’ve likely wrestled with how to structure your cleaning fee—especially if your properties are larger or more luxurious. In this episode of the Hosting Hotline, listener Penny called in with a great question about whether she should keep her cleaning fee separate on the invoice or roll it into her nightly rate. Penny operates two luxury short-term rentals in Hocking Hills, Ohio, under Woodland Retreats: the 10,000+ sq ft Hocking Manor and the 13,000+ sq ft Timber Frame Lodge with four separate apartments. These are large, unique, and luxurious properties—and understandably, the cleaning fee is significant. Here’s what we discussed on the episode to help Penny (and you!) make a smart, profitable decision. Step One: Know Your Costs First things first: you’ve got to know your actual cleaning costs. Whether you’re cleaning the properties yourself or using a vendor, it's essential to time the job, factor in laundry and supply costs, and calculate a fair labor rate. If you’re hiring it out, you can treat the cleaning fee as a pass-through expense (like we do). But if your cleaners are employees or in-house, it makes sense to build in some profit margin. Don’t guess. Don’t estimate. Know your numbers. Step Two: Understand Guest Expectations Cleaning fees are a sensitive topic for many travelers. That doesn’t mean you can’t charge a fair rate—it just means that how you present it matters. Gu

title: Are Your Minimum Stay Rules Costing You Bookings?
description: Are Minimum Stay Rules Costing You Bookings? If you're a short-term rental host wondering why bookings have slowed down, this episode is for you. Hosts everywhere are reporting fewer reservations—but when we take a peek behind the curtain, the culprit is often hiding in plain sight: minimum stay rules. While setting a two- or three-night minimum might feel like you're protecting your time, it could actually be blocking your profitability. Minimum Stay Rules Could Be Hurting You Airbnb and other booking platforms reward availability. If your listing isn’t bookable for the dates travelers are searching, you're automatically pushed out of results—and missing opportunities. Let’s say a guest wants to stay for two nights, but your listing requires a four-night minimum. You won’t even show up in their search. Even worse? If you have two existing bookings with a two-night gap between them, and your minimum stay is four nights, those two nights are now unbookable. Every unbooked night is money left on the table. What Hosts Get Wrong About Stay Restrictions Many hosts assume longer minimums will make their lives easier or reduce wear and tear. And while that may be true, it often leads to fewer bookings and lower occupancy. Here’s the tough love: if your goal is profitability, your calendar needs to reflect guest demand—not personal preference. That means understanding: What average stays look like in your market Whether you’re creating calendar gaps with your current settings If your

title: Can I Still Use StayFi with Airbnb’s New Rules?
description: Can You Still Use StayFi with Airbnb’s New Policy Updates? Here's What Hosts Need to Know When Airbnb announced its new Off-Platform and Fee Transparency Policy rolling out in May 2025, many short-term rental hosts had immediate questions about what it meant for guest communication—and third-party tools like StayFi. One of our Hosting Hotline listeners, Natasha, called in with a smart question: Are hosts still allowed to collect guest emails and phone numbers when they check in via WiFi using StayFi? We brought in Arthur Colker, CEO of StayFi , to help us break it down. Here’s what you need to know. What Airbnb’s New Policy Says About Guest Communication Airbnb’s updated policy says that hosts cannot: Sell, share, or use guest contact information without consent Add guests to marketing lists without permission At first glance, this language raised concerns for hosts who use tools like StayFi to build their guest email lists for direct bookings. StayFi’s Approach: Opt-In = Compliance Here’s the key: opt-in consent. According to Arthur, StayFi’s platform has always required guests to actively opt in when they log onto the WiFi splash page. They are not automatically added to a list. Instead, guests voluntarily provide their information, agreeing to receive communications from you. This opt-in process keeps hosts compliant with: Airbnb’s communication policies U.S. and international email marketing laws (CAN-SPAM, GDPR, etc.) As Arthur pointed out, the updated Airbnb policy lang

description: Pricing Isn’t Just Math—It’s Strategy: Wendy Doris on Revenue Management That Works If you've ever second-guessed your Airbnb pricing strategy or felt like dynamic pricing software is a mystery you’ll never solve—you’re not alone. This week on the podcast, we sat down with Arizona-based co-host and STR educator Wendy Doris of Mostess , who shared how learning to truly manage her revenue transformed her short-term rental business. This episode is packed with real-life stories, honest takeaways, and a serious dose of motivation to get into your numbers and stop leaving money on the table. From DIY Pricing to Data-Driven Revenue Strategy Wendy started just like many hosts do—picking a weekday and weekend rate, maybe bumping it up for high season, and calling it a day. But once she added a few more properties to her portfolio, it became clear that her pricing approach wasn’t scalable—or effective. Enter: dynamic pricing tools like PriceLabs. At first, Wendy trusted the tool to do the heavy lifting. But over time, she realized software alone wasn’t enough— she needed a strategy to interpret the data and make smart decisions across her 17 properties. “I started to see how much I was leaving on the table in April—thousands of dollars—because I hadn’t realized it was still a peak season in Phoenix,” Wendy said. title: Should I Add a Safe to My Airbnb?
description: Should You Add a Safe to Your Airbnb? Here’s What Hosts Need to Know Valerie called into the Hosting Hotline with a great question: she’s looking for a programmable guest safe—like the kind you find in hotel rooms—that guests can use to secure their valuables during their stay. But when she tried to find one online, it wasn’t as straightforward as she expected. We love this question because offering a safe is a thoughtful amenity that can elevate the guest experience—especially in shared spaces or properties with higher nightly rates. But like any amenity, it comes with operational considerations. Here’s what we walked through with Valerie: 1. Find the Right Safe We found a highly rated Amazon Basics security safe that mimics hotel functionality with a programmable keypad. Bonus: it’s under $150 and easy to return if it’s not a fit. 2. Guest Communication Is Key Make sure instructions are crystal clear. Include photos (and even a video) in your digital or printed guidebook that explain how to use the safe, how to set the code, and what to do if they forget the code. 3. Mounting and Accessibility Safes should be securely mounted to prevent theft. Be mindful of placement—guests of all sizes and ages should be able to access it easily. Think about clearance for opening the door fully and any furniture nearby. 4. What If They Forget the Code? You’ll need a backup access plan. Whether it’s a master key stored securely on-site or with your team, make sure you’re not caught off guar

After decades of running an interior architecture and design firm, Janice found herself navigating a major life transition. With resilience, creativity, and a deep understanding of hospitality, she transformed her personal property into a direct booking success story. In this episode of Thanks for Visiting , Janice shares her incredible journey from design firm owner to sought-after STR host in Abingdon, Virginia. Along the way, she faced challenges familiar to many hosts: limited local resources, the steep learning curve of property management, and the complexities of marketing directly to guests. title: What’s the Best Bedding for Airbnbs That Won’t Snag or Wrinkle?
description: What Sheets Are Best for Airbnb Hosts? A Practical Guide from the Hosting Hotline If you’re tired of snagged sheets, wrinkle-filled beds, and lint rolling every turnover, you’re not alone. This week’s Hosting Hotline question comes from Sarah in Redwood, Mississippi, who’s hosted over 30 stays in just six months—and wants sheets that actually hold up . Before you swap out your linens again, here are the top things to check first: 1. It’s Not Always the Sheets Issues like dullness or snagging can often come from: – Aging washers and dryers – Harsh or incompatible detergents – Poor water quality (especially hard water) Don’t overlook these basics—your linens might be great, but your equipment may be working against you. 2. Two Brands We Recommend – Standard Textile (via HostGPO): Used by Hilton and Marriott, designed for high-turnover hospitality use. Comes pre-washed in Tide. Available in poly blends and cotton. – California Design Den: Offers sample sets and a free program for STR hosts. Founder interview drops next week—don’t miss it! 3. Poly vs. Cotton: What’s Best? Cotton feels luxurious, but wrinkles. Poly blends resist wrinkles, but may feel less high-end. Want wrinkle-resistant cotton? Take your sheets out of the dryer just before they’re fully dry and smooth them by hand. 4. Lint Happens—Here’s What to Do There’s no magic bedding that prevents all lint or hair. Even luxury hotels still lint-roll beds as part of SOPs. Our top tips: – Lint roll after making the bed – Kee

What started as a side hustle turned into a full-fledged, six-figure hosting business with nine beautifully designed units in Houston, Texas. But the road was anything but easy. When a chance encounter introduced her to the world of Airbnb—via a woman with paint on her hands and passion in her voice—she followed her gut and said yes to helping stage and co-host a property. title: How to Furnish Your Short-Term Rental
description: How to Furnish Your Short-Term Rental (Even If You Have No Eye for Design) This week’s Hosting Hotline question comes from Joanna—a new host who describes herself as design-challenged but eager to make her short-term rental look professional, cohesive, and guest-ready. If you’re staring at an empty space and wondering if you should hire a designer, call a stager, or just wing it with Pinterest inspo, this post is for you. Before you spend a dime furnishing your property, here’s what to consider: 1. Good Design is a Business Investment When it comes to STRs, design isn’t just about aesthetics—it directly impacts bookings and revenue. Even if you’re tempted to cut corners after buying the property, now’s not the time to skimp. A thoughtful, cohesive design: Grabs attention in crowded listings Justifies higher nightly rates Encourages repeat bookings and glowing reviews 2. Should You Hire a Designer, Stager, or Friend? Not all design help is created equal. Here’s how to think about your options: Interior Designer (STR-specific): The gold standard. They understand function, durability, and guest expectations. Look for firms like Somerled Designs , who specialize in STRs and balance aesthetics with ROI. Traditional Home Stager: Caution here. Staging for resale is very different than staging for hospitality. Most stagers aren’t thinking about guest comfort, durability, or repeat use. description: A Deep Dive with California Design Den Founder Deepak Mehrotra As a host, you probably don’t spend your nights thinking about sheets—but your guests do. From wrinkled beds to stained pillowcases and scratchy duvets, the bed is one of the first places guests notice if something’s off. In this week’s episode, we sat down with Deepak Mehrotra of California Design Den —a bedding brand with 85,000+ Amazon reviews and a major following in the STR world. His goal? Help hosts deliver a luxury sleep experience that lasts (and doesn’t cost a fortune). 🎉 Want better sheets at a better price? 👉 Join California Design Den’s VIP Program for STR Hosts – Includes free sheet samples, exclusive pricing, and easy reordering. For questions, contact Satya directly at vip@californiadesignden.com . Here’s what we learned: 1. 400 Thread Count Is the Sweet Spot You don’t need 1000 thread count sheets—and they’re not always better. A 400 thread count cotton sheet offers the perfect balance of softness, durability, and price. They’re also lighter and easier to wash. 2. Microfiber Might Be Ruining Your Guest Experience Microfiber = plastic. It attracts dust, clings to hair, doesn’t breathe, and traps odors. While it may resist wrinkles and cost less, it’s no match for 100% cotton when it comes to guest comfort and long-term skin health. 3. Poly/Cotton Sheets? title: Curious About Midterm Rentals? What to Know Before You List
description: Is Your Airbnb Midterm-Ready? A Beginner’s Guide to 30+ Day Rentals Wondering if it’s time to pivot your short-term rental strategy? Midterm rentals—stays of 30 days or longer—are gaining serious traction, and in this week’s episode, we unpack why. Whether it’s to deal with seasonality, local STR regulations, or just burnout from constant turnovers, more and more hosts are dipping their toes into the midterm space. And thanks to platforms like Furnished Finder, it’s easier than ever to test the waters. 1. What Is a Midterm Rental? A stay of 30 days or longer, typically for business, medical, relocation, or lifestyle reasons. These guests want a furnished space with a “home away from home” vibe—but they’re not on vacation. 2. Pros of Midterm Rentals: Less turnover = fewer cleans and more stable income Longer stays mean less guest messaging No nightly price wars to stay competitive Ideal for hosts with slow seasons or tighter regulations 3. Key Differences From STRs: Unlike short stays, midterm guests often become legal tenants. That means credit checks, background screenings, and signing a lease (especially depending on your state). And unlike long-term renters, these guests still expect hospitality touches—just not the over-the-top amenities that dominate the STR world. 4. How to Know if Your Market Can Support It Check: FurnishedFinder.com/stats for real-time demand PriceLabs market dashboards (filter for 30+ day stays) Other listings in your area advertising monthly rates Y

title: Lessons from the UK: 4 Industry Experts on What Hosts Are Getting Right and Wrong
description: A Global Perspective on Hosting from the Thanks for Visiting Team This week’s episode takes us across the pond to Knowsley Hall, a 600-year-old estate now offering five-star hospitality experiences—and the backdrop for one of the most inspiring short-term rental gatherings we’ve attended. Surrounded by international founders and operators, we sat down with four leaders shaping the future of STR tech and hosting. Here's what stood out: 1. Smart Hosts Know Their ROI Ela from Autohost says the difference between thriving and flailing hosts often comes down to reassessing ROI—constantly. Whether it’s a tech tool, team member, or even the shampoo in your unit, if it’s not helping you earn or save money, it's time to cut it. 2. Ranking on OTAs Is a Science (and a Game) Bart from Autorank gave us a crash course in OTA visibility: – Open up your calendar, even on “undesirable” nights – Don’t rely solely on Airbnb—your ranking is affected by all OTAs – Regularly update your descriptions and photos for seasonality and SEO – Stop blocking gap nights unless you have to—they’re dragging you down 3. Direct Bookings Can Double Your Profit Phil from Futurestay makes a strong case: if your margins are 15–30%, OTA fees can wipe you out. Just 10–20% of your bookings coming in direct can double your profit. His advice? Start small, nurture past guests, and build slowly. It’s not about replacing OTAs—it’s about being more profitable. 4. “Kill Your Darlings” and Get Focused Marc from Boostly says

title: Should You Add a Security Camera to Your STR?
description: How to Confidently Handle Security in Your Short-Term Rental If you’re launching your first STR, figuring out how to handle security can be a little overwhelming. Do you need a camera? How do you handle smart locks without annoying your guests? In this Hosting Hotline episode, we hear from listener Winn, who’s preparing her very first listing in a quiet, rural area. Her questions are thoughtful—and incredibly common among new hosts: 1. Should I add a Ring camera? 2. Do I need to change the keypad code for every guest? Sarah and Annette break it all down, offering practical advice that balances guest experience with property protection . Here’s what we cover: – The first call you should make before installing any camera (hint: it’s your insurance provider) – Pros and cons of Ring-style doorbell cams and how guests typically perceive them – Smart lock recommendations that let you control access without requiring guests to download apps – Manual vs. automated code changes—and why even a single-property host should consider rotating codes after every stay – Clear communication tips to help guests feel safe, not surveilled One of the biggest takeaways? You don’t have to get it all perfect upfront. You can always add, update or remove security measures if they aren't working for you or your guests. Whether you’re hosting in a remote area or an urban neighborhood, this episode will help you make confident, informed decisions—without overthinking it. Want step-by-step help with your

title: Unlocking the STR Tax Loophole with Amanda Han, CPA
description: Unlocking the STR Tax Loophole with Amanda Han, CPA If you’ve been on social media for more than a few minutes, you’ve probably seen some version of this advice: “Want to pay less taxes? Buy a short-term rental!” But is it really that simple? In episode 465 of the Thanks for Visiting podcast, we sit down with Amanda Han, CPA and tax strategist, to demystify the so-called STR tax loophole and show short-term rental owners what they can (legally) do to reduce their tax burden. What is the STR Tax Loophole? The short-term rental tax loophole refers to a unique opportunity in the tax code that allows certain STR owners to claim real estate losses without qualifying as a real estate professional. That’s huge—because the traditional path to using real estate losses to offset W2 income requires proving that real estate is your primary profession. The STR loophole sidesteps this requirement if you meet specific material participation criteria. What is Material Participation? Material participation is different from being a real estate professional. Amanda explains that if your STR’s average guest stay is 7 days or less , the IRS classifies it as a business rather than a rental activity. This means you can claim losses against your other income—as long as you materially participate in managing the property. title: Are You Leaving Money on the Table by Not Offering Gap Nights?
description: Should You Offer Guests an Extra Night? Here’s When (and How) to Ask Gap nights—also known as adjacent or in-between nights—can be a goldmine for hosts, but only if you know when and how to offer them. Joanna called into the Hosting Hotline with a question we hear more and more: what’s the best timing to offer an extra night to guests—either before they arrive or after they depart? Also called “gap nights” or “adjacent nights,” these are open calendar days that sit before or after a confirmed booking. Joanna’s running a drive-to property in Central Virginia, and like many hosts, she’s looking for ways to increase midweek bookings and boost revenue without overhauling her business model. Here’s what we covered in the episode—and why we think every host should be offering these extra stays. Start with Your Mindset It’s easy to feel awkward about upselling, but offering an extra night isn’t about being salesy—it’s about hospitality. Guests are already choosing to stay with you. If there’s a chance to ease their arrival or extend their relaxation, why wouldn’t you offer it? Whether it’s arriving a night early to set up for a family event or staying late to enjoy Sunday morning coffee without a rush, you’re giving guests more of what they already want: a seamless, stress-free experience. When to Make the Offer There’s no one-size-fits-all answer—but here are the three windows we recommend testing: Right after booking: Especially if the guest shares why they’re visiting (wedding, r

title: Is Airbnb Just the Beginning? A Conversation with Phil Kennard of FutureStay
description: The Future of Direct Bookings with Phil Kennard of FutureStay Independent hosts are the backbone of the short-term rental industry—but many are still building their businesses on someone else’s platform. If that’s you, you’re not alone. But it’s time to think bigger. In this week’s episode of Thanks for Visiting , we sit down with Phil Kennard , the founder of FutureStay , a direct booking platform designed specifically for what he calls “rental-preneurs.” These are hosts who want more freedom, more control, and more profit from their properties—but without the complexity of traditional PMS systems. Phil shares: How he went from accidental host to building software for 100,000+ hosts Why over 90% of short-term rental hosts manage five or fewer properties What FutureStay does differently from property management software How to optimize your Airbnb listing with his “48-point checklist” Why even a few direct bookings can completely change your profitability The key to building trust with guests in what he calls the “trust recession” We also talk about how hosts can legally nurture guest relationships even under Airbnb’s new off-platform policy, what the “billboard effect” is, and how to show up on Google Vacation Rentals as your official website —not just a link back to a big OTA. If you’re ready to stop guessing and start building a more profitable, independent hosting business, this episode is a must-listen. Download a transcript of this episode . Resources: FutureStay | Dire

title: Hosting Hotline: Can You Ask Guests to Book Direct After Their Stay?
description: What Airbnb’s New Messaging Rules Mean for Direct Bookings What Airbnb’s New Messaging Rules Mean for Your Direct Booking Strategy As short-term rental hosts, many of us have relied on a simple tactic to encourage repeat guests: sending a friendly post-stay message through the Airbnb platform. It’s been common to include a review request and a nudge to book direct next time. But Airbnb’s latest policy update makes one thing clear: this method is no longer allowed. In this Hosting Hotline episode, listener Colby—who manages high-end properties in Australia—asks whether post-stay messages that encourage direct bookings are still compliant. The answer? A hard no… if you’re using Airbnb’s messaging system. So, what changed? While Airbnb has always discouraged promoting off-platform transactions, the company is now enforcing those guidelines with more scrutiny. Their AI actively monitors message content for violations—including direct booking prompts. Here’s what you need to know: ❌ What not to do: Don’t include direct booking links, discount codes, or promotional language in any message sent through Airbnb, even post-checkout. Don’t ask guests to leave reviews anywhere other than Airbnb’s built-in review platform. Don’t offer perks or upgrades in exchange for personal information. ✅ What’s still okay: You can ask for a 5-star Airbnb review—but keep it simple. You can request feedback via Airbnb messaging, as long as it doesn’t lead them off-platform. You can move communication to

title: Hosting Through a Crisis with Catherine Morris of Edenwood NC
description: How This Host Rebuilt Her Business—and Her Systems—After a Hurricane Catherine Morris didn’t expect a hurricane to hit her mountain. As the owner of Edenwood NC—a pair of thoughtfully designed vacation rentals outside Asheville—Catherine had built a hands-on, full-time hosting business from the ground up. She managed everything herself: cleaning, messaging, inspections, guest prep. But when Hurricane Helene swept through Western North Carolina, she was forced to navigate a situation no host wants to face: disaster mid-stay. In this episode, Catherine walks us through the reality of hosting during a regional emergency—what worked, what didn’t, and what she wishes she had in place before the storm hit. From Corporate Life to Full-Time Host Like many hosts, Catherine’s journey began with the search for a better work-life balance. After spending years in corporate America, she and her husband purchased six acres on their own mountain in hopes of creating a more flexible lifestyle. The result: two custom-built properties—the Barn, featuring a 150-gallon loft soaking tub, and the Treehouse, complete with a cedar wood-fired hot tub and views of Mount Pisgah. Both homes were designed exclusively for short-term rental, and Catherine chose to self-manage every guest-facing detail: cleaning, inspecting, messaging, and maintaining. title: Hosting Hotline: Should You Trade Free Stays for Influencer Content?
description: Should You Trade Free Stays for Influencer Content? Here’s How to Decide Influencer collaborations can be a smart marketing move for short-term rental hosts—especially for highly themed or photo-worthy properties. But are they worth it? And how do you protect yourself from wasted time, awkward interactions, or unclear ROI? That’s exactly what listener Julia asked in this week’s Hosting Hotline episode. She runs an immersive Star Wars-themed rental in Kissimmee, Florida, and after hosting a content creator through Stay Yamo, she saw a few new followers but no immediate bookings. So she called in wondering—how do I know if these collaborations are actually worth it? Here’s our advice: 1. Not All Influencers Are Equal Just because someone has a large following doesn’t mean they’re a good fit. Make sure their audience aligns with your property. Are their followers likely to book your space? Do they create high-quality content you actually want to use? If the answer is no, the collaboration likely won’t pay off. 2. Decide What Success Looks Like Before Saying Yes Are you hoping for bookings? Brand awareness? Content assets to reuse on your channels? Be clear on your goals. Some influencer partnerships are great for building awareness, others might deliver bookings—but very few do both. Set expectations upfront and know what you’re getting. 3. Red Flags Matter If someone reacts poorly when you decline a collaboration, consider it a blessing. Professional influencers understand that

title: Content Shoots at Your Airbnb: What to Ask, What to Charge, What to Avoid
description: Content Shoots at Your Airbnb: What to Ask, What to Charge, What to Avoid That DM in your inbox says: “Hi! I’m a local photographer and your space is gorgeous. Can I use it for a quick shoot?” Before you say yes, hit pause. Hosting content shoots at your Airbnb can be an amazing way to earn extra revenue, build relationships, and get your property noticed—but only if you protect yourself, your space, and your time. In this episode of, we’re sharing everything we’ve learned from years of hosting photography and production shoots in our properties. From small influencer swaps to full-blown commercial shoots, we’ve seen the good, the bad, and the awkward—and we’re laying it all out for you. Types of Content You Might Be Asked to Host Not all content shoots are created equal. It’s important to ask exactly what is being filmed and how your space will be used. Some examples: Influencer swaps Brand photography Commercial shoots Documentaries or film projects Boudoir sessions Adult content You have a right to know what’s happening inside your space—and to decide what aligns with your brand and comfort level. The Must-Have: A Certificate of Insurance (COI) This is non-negotiable . If the person or company using your space doesn’t have proper insurance coverage, don’t move forward. A COI protects you and your property in case of damage or injury—and any reputable production team should be able to provide one quickly. Ask These Questions Before Saying Yes We recommend creating your own

title: Hosting Hotline: Is Dynamic Pricing Worth It for Just One Seasonal Property?
description: Should You Use PriceLabs If You Only Have One Seasonal Property? If you’re a short-term rental host with a seasonal property, you’ve probably found yourself wondering if dynamic pricing tools like PriceLabs are really worth the investment. After all, if you’re not booking year-round, can a $20/month software really move the needle? This week on the Hosting Hotline, we’re diving into that exact question from a listener who runs a single seasonal property and wants to know if PriceLabs can help her optimize her bookings. Our take? Yes—especially for seasonal hosts. Here’s why: When your calendar only fills up during certain parts of the year, every available night becomes that much more important. You don’t have the luxury of underpriced weekends or last-minute gaps. PriceLabs gives you unbiased, market-informed insights into what other properties are charging, what’s getting booked, and what adjustments you can make in real-time to stay competitive. Whether it’s adjusting minimum night stays, spotting trends, or capitalizing on local events, dynamic pricing tools help you work smarter—not harder. And when just one extra booking can cover your monthly software cost (and then some), the ROI quickly becomes obvious. So if you’ve been pricing manually or relying on Airbnb’s Smart Pricing, it might be time to level up with a tool that puts the power back in your hands. And if you’re not ready to commit? Good news—PriceLabs offers a free trial with no credit card required. Bottom li

title: How Taylor Gates Scaled from Golf Cart Tours to Redefining Vacation Rentals
description: Building a Scalable STR Brand Without Losing the Guest Experience What does it take to scale a short-term rental business from one listing to a full-blown hospitality brand? For Taylor Gates, it started with one favor for a neighbor and a borrowed golf cart. What followed was over a decade of intentional growth, operational discipline, and a relentless focus on the guest experience. In this episode of Thanks for Visiting , Taylor breaks down how he went from managing a few rooms on Airbnb to building Stay Duvet—one of Charleston’s leading short-term rental brands—and launching Fluffy, a commercial laundry and housekeeping service designed specifically for STRs. Here’s what hosts will take away from this episode: 1. It All Starts With Service—Not Scale Taylor’s foundation in hospitality came from his family’s steakhouse business. That early exposure to service shaped everything: from how he talks to homeowners to how he trains housekeepers. Whether you’re managing one unit or 100, this episode is a reminder that the guest experience should never be treated as an afterthought. Taylor shares how thinking like a guest—not just a host—transformed his approach to design, comfort, and operations. 2. Cleanliness Is a Non-Negotiable—and an Opportunity You’ll hear Taylor’s full breakdown of what goes into his clean duvet guarantee , why he refuses to use microfiber sheets, and how a well-laundered bed can drastically improve reviews and guest loyalty. title: Hosting Hotline: How Should You Handle TVs and Streaming Services in Your Rental?
description: Are You Managing TVs and Streaming Services the Right Way in Your Short-Term Rental? In this Hosting Hotline episode, listener Amy from Canada brings up an increasingly important topic for short-term rental hosts: how to handle TVs and streaming services in a way that enhances the guest experience while staying secure and easy to manage. It’s not just about mounting a screen and calling it a day. From TV placement to app logins, this episode breaks down what you need to consider when building your property's in-home entertainment plan. Here are a few key takeaways for hosts: 1. TV Placement Is Part of the Guest Experience While some hosts may choose to limit TVs to the living room, guests often expect to have a TV in every bedroom—especially when traveling with others. Whether it's couples wanting to unwind separately or kids watching a movie while the adults relax, hosts should consider TVs as part of a hospitality-first setup. With screen prices more affordable than ever, this is a small investment that can improve guest satisfaction and reduce booking hesitation. 2. Don’t Overpromise Streaming Access When it comes to apps, simpler is better. If you only offer Netflix, make that clear in your listing. If your property is in a sports-heavy market or you're marketing to families, it might be worth offering Disney+, Hulu Live, or YouTube TV seasonally or year-round. Align your subscriptions with your guest demographics—and don’t forget to audit your costs. 3. Security and Simp

title: Airbnb Damage Claims 101
description: Airbnb Damage Claims 101: A Host’s Guide to Getting Reimbursed (Without Losing Your Cool) Accidents happen. Sometimes it’s a wine glass, sometimes it’s a chandelier (true story)—but either way, damage is part of the short-term rental business. The question is: how do you handle it? In this post, we’re diving into the essentials of Airbnb damage claims. We’ll walk you through how to decide what qualifies as a claim, how to submit one through Airbnb’s AirCover, and how to communicate with your guests in a way that’s both professional and effective. Let’s make damage claims less dramatic—and more manageable. First: What Actually Counts as “Damage”? Not everything that breaks, stains, or goes missing is worth filing a claim over. This is one of the biggest mindset shifts we want you to make as a host. Not a damage claim: Makeup stains on towels or sheets A missing washcloth A broken wine glass A game piece gone missing A scuff on the wall from a suitcase Dye on a towel from someone coloring their hair These are all frustrating, but they fall under wear and tear or cost of doing business. True damage (and worth filing a claim): A broken piece of furniture due to clear negligence Evidence of smoking in a no-smoking property Significant staining or damage throughout the property Missing or damaged high-ticket items Vandalism or intentional destruction Damage that prevents the next guest from checking in Before You File a Claim, Ask Yourself: Is this negligent or accidental? title: Hosting Hotline: Do You Really Need an LLC to Start Hosting?
description: Do You Really Need an LLC to Start Hosting? Here’s What You Should Consider For new hosts entering the short-term rental space, the legal side of things can feel overwhelming—especially when it comes to forming an LLC. Listener Jennifer called into the Hosting Hotline with a simple but important question: Do I really need an LLC to protect myself and my assets? In this episode, we walk through what an LLC actually does, who it protects, and how to decide whether it’s the right move for your hosting business. While we’re not lawyers or accountants, we share practical insights based on our own experiences—and outline the three key professionals you should bring into the conversation. Whether you’re about to launch your first property or you’ve been operating without a formal structure for a while, this episode will help you understand: Why your long-term goals should inform your legal structure The potential benefits of liability protection How an LLC can streamline your operations and mindset as a business owner Alternative strategies to discuss with your CPA and insurance broker Why you shouldn’t wait until “you’re bigger” to get set up correctly Even if you’ve already started hosting, it’s never too late to evaluate your business setup and take steps to protect your future growth. Tune in and walk away with a clearer picture of how to move forward with confidence. Resources mentioned: – Proper Insurance : The go-to insurance provider built specifically for short-term rental

title: Fix Your Airbnb Title: Simple Changes That Drive More Bookings
description: Why Your Airbnb Listing Title Isn’t Working—and How to Write One That Books This week on Thanks for Visiting , Sarah and Annette are tackling one of the most important (and overlooked) elements of your short-term rental: your listing title. You only get 50 characters—but those 50 characters are some of the most powerful marketing real estate on your Airbnb page. If your title doesn’t grab attention and clearly communicate value, you're missing out on clicks, bookings, and ultimately, revenue. In this episode, Sarah and Annette break down the common title mistakes hosts make (yes, “cozy,” “retreat,” and “charming” have to go), and walk you through what actually works in today’s competitive short-term rental landscape. They share practical examples, real host case studies, and offer a repeatable system to help you write titles that drive more traffic—and convert it. Here’s what you’ll learn: – Why your Airbnb title is your digital billboard—and how to use it effectively – Common words that are silently killing your listing (and what to say instead) – The importance of guest-focused language and pulling from your reviews – How to stack amenities and highlight unique features that catch attention – Smart ways to rotate your title for events, seasons, and guest intent – How to test and track title performance so you're not flying blind Sarah and Annette also offer their favorite tip for when you're stuck on what to write: use your reviews. Your past guests are already telling you

title: Hosting Hotline: Can You Actually Sell Your STR as a Turnkey Business?
description: Thinking About Selling Your STR? Here’s What Hosts Need to Know If you’ve ever thought about selling your short-term rental, you’ve probably wondered—how much is it actually worth? And can I sell it as a functioning, revenue-generating business? In this Hosting Hotline episode, Sarah and Annette answer Lori’s thoughtful question about preparing an STR for sale and explore what it takes to position your property as more than just a home. The first thing to understand? STRs are almost always appraised as residential real estate—not businesses. That means your furniture, future bookings, and even five-star Airbnb reviews aren’t automatically baked into the price. But that doesn’t mean they don’t have value. Here’s what you can do to maximize your potential sale price and make the process smoother: Find a STR-savvy realtor: Your agent should understand the STR landscape, be connected to investor buyers, and know how to market a turnkey furnished rental. Build out business assets: A direct booking website, guest email list (via tools like StayFi), and strong social following all add to your property's appeal—especially for investors looking for plug-and-play businesses. Keep detailed performance records: Historical booking data, occupancy rates, and revenue numbers help buyers understand income potential—even if your current margins aren’t stellar. Consider creative deals: If your future bookings are tied to your personal Airbnb account, you might offer to co-host for a few months

title: Are You Actually Covered? What Hosts Need to Know About Insurance with Darren Nix from Steadily
description: Are You Overlooking the One Thing That Could Sink Your STR Business? When it comes to short-term rentals, most hosts are laser-focused on booking calendars, five-star reviews, and boosting nightly rates. But what about insurance? In this episode of Thanks for Visiting, Darren Nix, founder of landlord insurance company Steadily , joins us to explain why so many hosts are underinsured—and what to do about it. Steadily – Landlord insurance built for real estate investors. Darren shares his own hard-earned story: a rental property fire that led to $250,000 in damages and a frustrating, confusing claims process that dragged on for nearly a year. That experience set him on a mission to build an insurance company built around real estate investors—people who understand that rental properties are small businesses, not just homes. So what do most hosts get wrong? Here are some highlights: Fires, water damage, and wind—not guests—are the biggest insurance risks. Think less about party damage and more about burst pipes or trees crashing through your roof. A 30-day vacancy can void your coverage. Yep, that’s real. And most hosts don’t find out until it’s too late. If your property sits vacant for too long—even unintentionally—you may not be covered for certain claims. Aircover is not a full insurance policy. It only covers guest-caused damage during the stay. If anything else happens (fire, storm, vandalism), it won’t protect you. You might be underinsured and not know it. Many hosts hav

title: Hosting Hotline: Are You Really Covered by Airbnb Insurance?
description: Is Airbnb’s AirCover Really Protecting Your STR? Here's What Hosts Need to Know Many hosts operate under the false assumption that Airbnb’s AirCover provides full insurance coverage. Unfortunately, that couldn’t be further from the truth—and it could leave you vulnerable to costly damages or income loss. In this Hosting Hotline episode, we answer a question from one of our YouTube subscribers: Is Airbnb’s liability promise enough? The short answer: no. While AirCover sounds official, it’s not an insurance policy in your name—and it doesn’t cover many of the things you might think it does. We cover why relying on basic homeowners insurance is also a risky move. Most standard policies don’t allow for short-term rental activity, and if your insurance company finds out, they may cancel your coverage or deny your claims entirely. Damage from guests? Loss of income due to weather events? Many hosts find out too late that they’re not covered at all. We also talk about: Real examples of denied AirCover claims Why guest-related damages aren’t even the biggest issue—natural events are What to look for in a proper STR insurance policy Why having a direct booking site and diversifying platforms protects your income As hosts ourselves, we’ve seen the value of working with STR-specialized insurers who understand this business. Companies like Proper and Steadily offer policies built specifically for short-term rentals—and give you actual peace of mind. Finally, we recommend reviewing your p

title: Are Influencer Stays Worth It? How to Know, What to Ask, and When to Say No
description: Should You Partner with Influencers? What Every Host Needs to Know Before Saying Yes It starts with a flattering DM: “Hey! I love your space and would love to collaborate…” Before you block off your peak weekend and offer up a free stay, take a beat. In this episode of Thanks for Visiting , we’re unpacking the real truth behind influencer marketing for short-term rentals. Hosts are often unsure of how to respond to influencer requests—especially when there's a promise of content, exposure, and bookings, but no guarantee. Here’s what we cover: 1. Influencer Marketing Is Still Marketing That means it needs strategy, goals, and clear expectations. Are you looking for content? Brand awareness? Bookings? Start there. 2. It’s Not All About the Follower Count An influencer with 2,000 loyal followers who trust their recommendations can outperform someone with 200,000 disengaged fans. Engagement rate matters—and so does audience alignment. 3. Define the Deliverables Don’t assume they’ll tag you or send you the photos. Be specific. What kind of content are they producing? When will you receive it? Can you repost it later? 4. Always Have a Contract Even if it’s a micro-influencer staying one night, protect your business with a clear agreement. Include cancellation policies, usage rights, deliverables, and even insurance requirements. 5. title: Hosting Hotline: How Much Control Is Too Much? Temp Settings and Turnover Tips
description: How Much Control Should You Really Give Your Guests? Let’s Talk Couch Covers and Thermostats When it comes to setting up your short-term rental, every detail matters—including your furniture choices and thermostat settings. In this episode of the Hosting Hotline, we hear from Dayne, a new host who just took over management of his mom’s two properties. He asks two great questions: 1) Are couch cushion covers worth the hassle? And 2) Should you restrict the thermostat so guests can’t crank it too high or too low? Sarah and Annette weigh in with practical advice and lived experience. When it comes to couches, they recommend investing in quality performance fabric that’s easy to spot clean and resists staining—versus removable covers that add time to turnovers and may make guests question what’s underneath. As for temperature control, the hosts advocate for giving guests the freedom to make themselves comfortable—while using smart thermostat features like eco mode and motion detection to manage usage responsibly. They explain why guest comfort should always be the priority, even if it occasionally leads to quirky heating or cooling choices. Whether you're furnishing your rental or fine-tuning operations, this episode is packed with useful tips to make your STR more efficient and guest-friendly. title: Should You Choose a Strict or Flexible Airbnb Cancellation Policy?
description: Strict vs. Flexible: Which Airbnb Cancellation Policy Should I Choose? Choosing the right cancellation policy for your Airbnb listing can feel like a high-stakes decision. Go too strict and you risk losing bookings. Go too flexible and you may lose money when guests cancel last minute. In this episode, we’re unpacking the real-world implications of Airbnb’s cancellation policy options—from Flexible to Super Strict—and sharing how we approach guest refund requests in our own hosting business. If you’ve ever been unsure how to respond to a cancellation request or struggled with what’s “fair,” you’re not alone. We’re sharing examples of actual guest messages, the internal decision trees we use, and how we balance compassion with protecting our bottom line. What Are the Airbnb Cancellation Policy Options? Airbnb offers several cancellation policy tiers: Flexible – Full refund up to 24 hours before check-in Moderate – Full refund up to 5 days before check-in Firm – Full refund up to 30 days before check-in (partial after) Strict – 50% refund up to 7 days before check-in (with limited full-refund window) Super Strict – Application-only, specialty properties only Does Your Policy Affect Your Visibility on Airbnb? Many hosts wonder if choosing a more flexible policy will get them more bookings—or a better spot in Airbnb’s search results. While there’s no confirmed “boost” from Airbnb for any specific policy, what does matter is how well your cancellation policy aligns with your prope

title: Hosting Hotline: How Can You Monetize Your Property’s Most Unique Feature?
description: Turning Unique Amenities Into Revenue: Why Your Escape Room Is More Than Just a Perk This week on the Hosting Hotline, listener Mike from Hocking Hills, Ohio, called in with a smart question about whether he could sync his Airbnb listing calendar with an Airbnb Experience. Mike recently added a full escape room to one of his properties, and wanted to open it up to guests even on days the cabin wasn’t booked. Here’s the short answer: No, Airbnb currently doesn’t allow calendar syncing between listings and experiences. But the real opportunity here? Rethinking what a unique amenity can do for your business. If you’ve created a stand-out feature for your STR—whether that’s an escape room, pizza oven, wine tasting room, or backyard putting green—you don’t have to wait for Airbnb to help you monetize it. You can build your own ecosystem of guests, events, and marketing strategies to drive traffic and revenue on your own terms. Some of our favorite ideas from the episode: Host special event weeks focused on your feature (like escape room week for corporate teams or families) Use the amenity as a loss leader —draw people in with the experience, then get them excited to book an overnight stay Create a waitlist for the feature and open limited availability to generate buzz Remarket to past guests who loved the amenity, or offer referral incentives Partner with local influencers or communities to spread the word outside Airbnb The key takeaway? Don’t just think like a host—think like a

title: Chargebacks 101: Spotting Red Flags and Safeguarding Your Bookings
description: How to Protect Your Rental from Costly Chargebacks If you’re in the short-term rental game long enough, you’ll hear the term “chargeback.” And while it’s not the most glamorous topic, understanding how to prevent and handle chargebacks can save you hundreds—or even thousands—of dollars. A chargeback occurs when a guest disputes a charge with their credit card company, and the funds are pulled back from your account. When you’re hosting through an OTA like Airbnb, they’re the merchant of record —meaning they handle chargebacks. But if you book through Vrbo, Furnished Finder, or direct, you are the merchant of record, and it’s your responsibility to respond. In this episode of Thanks for Visiting , Sarah and Annette cover: Why chargebacks happen: – Fraudulent bookings with stolen credit cards – Guests claiming the property wasn’t as advertised – Post-stay disputes over amenities or service issues How to prevent them: Screen last-minute bookings carefully. Match ID to credit card name. Watch for multiple payment methods. Take note of evasive communication or pushy booking requests. Have a strong guest agreement and house rules. Perform thorough pre-arrival inspections. Create SOPs for conflict resolution. What to do if one happens: Act quickly—payment processors give you a short window to dispute. Gather evidence like signed agreements, communication logs, and lock code usage. Stay factual, concise, and professional in your response. While winning can be rare, persistence pays o

title: Hosting Hotline: Are You Doing Enough to Market Your Midterm Rental?
description: Why listing on Airbnb isn’t enough—and the daily actions that will actually fill your MTR calendar. If you own a midterm rental, simply having a listing on Airbnb or Furnished Finder isn’t enough to guarantee consistent bookings. In this episode of the Hosting Hotline, Kelsey from Arizona—who owns a Wisconsin MTR—asks how she can get more active in marketing her property. The truth? Listing on a couple of platforms is the bare minimum . Successful hosts treat their MTR like a business, with daily marketing actions and intentional tracking of results. Here are a few highlights from our conversation: 1. Build a referral system. Your past guests are one of your most valuable marketing channels. If they loved your place, offer an incentive for referring friends, coworkers, or family who might also need housing. 2. Make strategic local connections. Relationships with insurance companies, relocation agencies, HR departments, and universities can lead to high-value, repeat bookings. But they require follow-up—don’t just fill out a form and hope for the best. 3. Tap into community groups. Local Facebook groups (especially for travel nurses), real estate meetups, and professional associations are perfect places to share your property and build trust with potential guests or referral partners. 4. Get creative with publicity. From pitching local media to writing SEO-friendly blog content, there are ways to get your MTR in front of people who are already searching for housing in your are

title: Why Your Airbnb Listing Might Be in Trouble (and You Don’t Even Know It)
description: The Airbnb Feature You’re Not Checking (But Should Be) If bookings have slowed or your property seems buried in search results, it might be time to dig into a little-known section of your Airbnb dashboard: the Listing Issues tab. Hosts Sarah and Annette recently discovered this feature when one of their beautifully maintained properties was flagged by Airbnb. The issue? A guest tried to bring kittens —despite their clearly stated “dogs only” policy. Even though they handled the situation with care and refunded the guest, Airbnb marked it as a “forced cancellation,” triggering a warning on their account. That experience—and another involving a guest upset about not staying in a shared space—prompted this week’s episode, which breaks down what Airbnb’s listing issues mean and why they matter. Here are some of the biggest takeaways: Listing issues aren’t always obvious. They might come from AI, not human feedback. Private reviews count. Even if it doesn’t show up publicly, it could impact your ranking. Your PMS might show red/yellow/green lights. Those indicators reflect listing health and may hint at hidden demerits. Words matter. Even saying “cancel” in your messages can trigger Airbnb’s bots. Sarah and Annette’s advice? Take extreme ownership. Review your listing language, house rules, and communication style regularly. Check the listing issues tab weekly. And above all—don’t rely solely on Airbnb. Use this as your sign to diversify where and how you’re getting bookings. Beca

title: Hosting Hotline: What Happens to Bookings When You Buy a Short-Term Rental?
description: Why you can’t just inherit someone else’s Airbnb calendar—and how to handle bookings when buying an active STR. Buying a property that’s already an active short-term rental sounds like a dream—instant income, five-star reviews, and a ready-made calendar full of guests. But here’s the reality: short-term rental listings belong to the host , not the property. That means you can’t simply “inherit” someone else’s Airbnb or VRBO profile, even if the home stays exactly the same. In this Hosting Hotline episode, Renee calls in to ask how she should handle future bookings for a property she and her husband are buying. Sarah and Annette walk through the important considerations every buyer should know: 1. Know what you’re actually buying. Unless the sale includes the business (brand, website, social media, email list, merchant accounts), you’re buying the property only—not the booking infrastructure. 2. Decide how to handle existing bookings. Options include: Letting the seller keep their listings active to see current reservations through. Negotiating for guests to cancel and rebook with you (with OTA approval). Having the OTAs help move bookings to your new listing. 3. Understand the risks. Mixing old reservations with your new operations can cause problems—unclear responsibility for cleaning, guest communication, payouts, and even taxes. 4. Get everything in writing. From how funds are transferred to how cancellations are handled, put agreements on paper and have them signed by bot

title: Mastering Short-Term Rental Finances with REI Hub’s Adam Hamilton
description: How REI Hub Helps Hosts Take Control of Short-Term Rental Finances When it comes to running a short-term rental, most hosts put their energy into the guest experience—clean spaces, welcoming touches, five-star reviews. But behind the scenes, one of the most overlooked parts of hosting is also one of the most important: your finances. Without accurate bookkeeping, it’s nearly impossible to know if your rental is truly profitable. Many hosts focus on gross revenue—how much comes in each month—without accounting for the wide range of expenses that chip away at those earnings. From cleaning fees and utilities to repairs, supplies, and platform commissions, expenses add up fast. REI Hub — Bookkeeping software built for real estate investors and STR hosts. In this episode of Thanks for Visiting , we’re joined by Adam Hamilton, co-founder of REI Hub, who shares insights into the unique bookkeeping challenges that come with running a short-term rental. We cover: Why tracking more than just revenue matters. Profitability depends on understanding your full financial picture, not just the payout from your booking platform. The most common mistakes STR hosts make. From mixing personal and rental finances to forgetting deductible expenses, Adam outlines what to avoid. How clean books open doors. title: Hosting Hotline: Should You Team Up with Another Host?
description: The Benefits and Challenges of Partnering with Another Short-Term Rental Host One of the best parts of hosting is the creativity it allows—and listener Jan from Tucson brought us a fresh idea. After meeting a fellow host on her street, Jan started to imagine what would happen if they joined forces. Her home has a hot tub, her neighbor’s has a pool and putting green. What if guests could book both for the ultimate stay? It’s a great example of looking at your market differently. Rather than seeing each other as competition, these two hosts saw potential for collaboration. By combining properties, they could attract larger groups, expand amenities, and market themselves as a destination rather than just individual listings. But as we discussed in this episode, partnerships require more than just creative thinking. There are some important steps to consider before launching a joint listing: Liability and insurance: If a guest gets hurt at your neighbor’s pool while booking through you, what happens? Clear contracts and insurance coverage are essential. Revenue sharing: How will you split income fairly if both properties are involved? You may need to open a joint account or invoice one another. Calendar coordination: Both homes need to be available at the same time to make the combined listing work, which can complicate individual bookings. Consistency in guest experience: Your listing standards should match. Guests expect the same level of quality across both properties. That do

title: Stop Slashing Your Rates: Why Lower Prices Don’t Create Demand
description: Lowering your rates isn’t a strategy—here’s what to do instead. As a short-term rental host, it can feel tempting to hit the panic button when bookings slow down and drop your nightly rate. But here’s the truth: lowering your price doesn’t create demand. If travelers aren’t searching in your market, a cheaper rate won’t suddenly make them pack a suitcase. In this episode of Thanks for Visiting , Sarah and Annette dive into the myth of rate-cutting and explain why it’s hurting more hosts than helping. Demand is driven by guest search behavior—not how badly you need bookings. Instead of discounting, the duo shares practical strategies you can put into place today to make your listing stronger, stand out from competitors, and maximize profitability. From auditing your listing and amenities to improving your messaging and tightening up your calendar settings, Sarah and Annette give you a blueprint for truly “trying everything.” They’ll also walk you through how to take control of your business by building direct booking strategies, leveraging referrals, and creating your own demand outside of the Airbnb ecosystem. If you want to run a sustainable hosting business, it’s time to stop the race to the bottom and start pricing—and marketing—like a pro. Download a transcript of this episode . title: Hosting Hotline: How Should Hosts Respond to Airbnb’s New 15.5% Fee?
description: What the new 15.5% host-only fee structure it means for your short-term rental business—and how to respond strategically. Airbnb’s recent fee update has hosts buzzing. Starting October 27, 2025, PMS-connected hosts will be automatically switched to a single host-only fee of 15.5%. By December 1, “most hosts” not using PMS will follow. Understandably, this change has sparked a wave of questions: Should you raise rates? Leave Airbnb? Double down on direct bookings? In this Hosting Hotline episode, Sarah and Annette bring in TFV COO and revenue manager Colleen Prochaska to answer those questions head-on. Together, they unpack Airbnb’s fee shift, share real experiences from their own portfolio, and explain why this isn’t the end of the world for hosts—in fact, it’s an opportunity to tighten up your business strategy. From adjusting rates without losing occupancy to diversifying booking platforms, this episode will help you reframe the change and approach it like the business owner you are. The winners in this new landscape will be the hosts who know their numbers, optimize their booking funnels, and treat hosting as a business, not a hobby. Resources Booked & Profitable Bootcamp – Learn how to optimize your pricing, calendar, and booking funnel so you can thrive in any market. thanksforvisiting.com/bootcamp Airbnb Fee Structure Update – Read Airbnb’s official announcement here: https://www.airbnb.com/help/article/1857/service-fees Have More Questions? We’d love to hear from you!

title: How to Choose the Right PMS Without Losing Your Mind or Your Bookings
description: Ready to Get Serious About Hosting? Here’s How to Choose the Right PMS If short-term rental hosting is becoming more than a hobby for you, it’s time to start thinking like a business—and that starts with choosing the right property management system (PMS). A PMS is more than just a tech tool. It’s the operational hub of your hosting business. It automates guest messaging, prevents double bookings, helps you manage multiple channels (like Airbnb, VRBO, and your direct booking site), and even lets your team members log in and help you manage the day-to-day. In this episode, Sarah and Annette walk you through the exact questions to ask before choosing a PMS. They also cover: Why you should plan for the business you want , not just the one you have How PMS pricing works (and where sneaky onboarding fees may show up) How to test for good user experience —because if you hate logging in, you won’t use it Which core features to prioritize , from calendar syncing to payout reporting Advanced features like team permissions, accounting integrations, upsell tools, and AI If you’re still stuck in “analysis paralysis,” the biggest tip? Choose something and commit. No system is perfect, and you’ll always find workarounds or improvements down the line. Bonus tip: Once you’ve chosen a PMS, don’t rush the setup. Give yourself double the time they recommend and use that period to learn, test, and ask questions. Choosing a PMS is a big step in professionalizing your business. Make it count. Down

description: Growing a co-hosting business takes more than luck When it comes to scaling your short-term rental business, one of the biggest challenges is attracting new homeowners who trust you to manage their properties. This week on the Hosting Hotline, Sarah and Annette answer Susan’s question about how to grow a co-host portfolio and share the lessons they’ve learned from their own journey, as well as from the many co-hosts they’ve coached over the years. Building a thriving portfolio doesn’t happen overnight—it’s about creating consistent visibility, making the ask often, and building trust through professional communication and systems. From networking with real estate agents to sharing behind-the-scenes content on social media, the strategies discussed in this episode will help you put yourself in the right place, with the right message, at the right time. By the end, you’ll understand why tracking your outreach is key, how to nurture relationships for the long game, and how small steps can eventually lead to exponential growth. If you’re ready to expand your co-hosting business, this episode is packed with practical, actionable advice. title: How to Maximize Your Holiday Pricing Strategy for 2025
description: Strategic pricing now means more profit—and more peace—this holiday season. The 2025 holiday calendar is working in your favor, but only if you know how to use it. With Christmas and New Year’s Eve falling midweek and leading into bonus long weekends, travel demand will spike in ways many hosts won’t be prepared for. In this episode of Thanks for Visiting , Sarah and Annette share why holiday-specific pricing and minimums are crucial this year—and how you can set them up quickly and effectively. Here’s what you’ll learn: Why December 24–28 and December 31–January 4 should have longer minimum stays. How to capture bonus demand on December 26–28 and January 1–4 with small rate increases. Why dynamic pricing software makes holiday management stress-free. How to prepare your cleaning team and property so guest stays run smoothly. Why you should treat holidays as “revenue management holidays,” not just dates on the calendar. The key takeaway: don’t let 2025’s unique holiday travel patterns catch you off guard. With the right pricing strategy, you can maximize profit while still enjoying time off for yourself and your team. Download a transcript of this episode . title: Hosting Hotline: Do You Really Need Professional Photos for Your STR?
description: The debate over professional vs. DIY listing photos—and why the choice matters for your business. If you’ve been listening to us for a while, you know we don’t shy away from taking a strong stance. And one of the things we’ve always believed in? Professional photos. To us, it’s a non-negotiable if you want to run your short-term rental like a real business. But not everyone agrees—and that’s what makes this conversation so important. A listener recently commented that she’s never hired a professional photographer, and her guests consistently say her property looks just like the photos. In her eyes, that money is better spent on things like new bedding or more comfortable mattresses. So, is she right? In this Hosting Hotline, we unpack both sides of the debate. On one hand, professional photos have been proven to increase clicks, bookings, and average daily rates. They also send a strong signal to guests—and to yourself—that you’re serious about hosting. On the other hand, not all professional photos are created equal. If the images oversell your property or don’t reflect its current condition, they can hurt your credibility instead of helping it. Here’s what you’ll take away from this episode: How to use guest feedback (even when you disagree with it) to refine your marketing Why the best listing photos attract the right guests and repel the wrong ones The difference between investing in photos that elevate your brand versus photos that misrepresent your space Why professiona

title: How Kristen and Michael Built a Standout STR Brand in Joshua Tree
description: Lessons on risk, reinvestment, and branding from the hosts of The Seventh Ray House. Joshua Tree may be a crowded market, but Kristen and Michael prove that with the right mix of creativity, strategy, and storytelling, you can still stand out. Their journey into hosting started with risk—living in their property during renovations and betting on themselves when family members doubted the move. Four years later, The Seventh Ray House is thriving. In this episode of Thanks for Visiting, Sarah and Annette sit down with Kristen and Michael to uncover how they built a brand that goes beyond just another rental listing. Here are some highlights from their story: Starting small, reinvesting big: From wedding registry plates to a $7,500 hot tub, every upgrade was carefully timed for maximum ROI. Photography as a game changer: Hiring a professional photographer instantly elevated their listing and bookings. Direct booking strategy: Using Lodgify, they built a site that looks professional, is easy to manage, and helps reduce OTA dependence. Staying true to their vision: Instead of chasing every trend, they focused on creating a property and brand they’re genuinely proud of. Facing industry shifts: With the “gold rush” era over, they see the future of STRs belonging to hosts who provide real value and thoughtful design. Their biggest takeaway? Success in short-term rentals comes from combining passion with discipline—slowing down in the right moments so you can speed up long-term growth

title: Hosting Hotline: How Do You Build Trust Without Reviews?
description: Why “New Listing” Might Not Mean What You Think Launching a new short-term rental is exciting, but it also comes with a big challenge: earning guest trust without the cushion of glowing reviews. A recent Hosting Hotline question asked if hosts should write “new listing” in their title or description to explain why there are no reviews yet. Sarah and Annette’s advice? Use that “new” label carefully. While Airbnb gives new properties a boost in search results, guests may interpret “brand new” as meaning the home itself is newly renovated, fully updated, and spotless. If your property doesn’t live up to that expectation, you risk disappointing your first guests—something that can hurt your listing more than help it. Instead of relying on “new listing” alone, focus on: Accuracy. Highlight what’s genuinely fresh—renovations, new furniture, or a recent refresh. Responsiveness. Quick replies to guest inquiries build confidence before reviews do. Transparency. Meet objections head-on with language like, “Wondering why we don’t have many reviews yet? We’re new to Airbnb, but not to hospitality.” Preparedness. Deliver a seamless guest experience by pressure-testing your property and having backup systems in place. Your first reviews will set the tone for your hosting journey, so focus on creating an outstanding guest experience from day one. That way, the lack of reviews becomes less of a hurdle and more of a short-lived phase. Resources Lodgify – All-in-one software to manage bookings

title: The Truth About Midterm Rentals: What Hosts Need to Know Before Pivoting
description: Midterm rentals promise steady income—but the reality is far more complex The buzz around midterm rentals is hard to ignore. For many hosts, it sounds like the perfect solution to slow seasons or unpredictable booking windows—longer stays, fewer turnovers, and less work. But as Sarah and Annette share in this week’s episode, the truth is a little more complicated. After three years of running hybrid models that include both short- and midterm stays, they’ve learned that midterm rentals come with a unique set of operational, legal, and financial challenges that most hosts don’t anticipate. For starters, the income isn’t guaranteed. Guests on temporary work assignments or medical stays can cancel early or request unpredictable extensions. Then there’s the juggling act of keeping your listings visible across multiple platforms—Airbnb, Furnished Finder, and your own direct booking site—while trying to maintain a steady stream of reviews. Even the “fewer turnovers” myth doesn’t hold up under scrutiny. When guests live in your property for months, the cleanings become deeper, more time-consuming, and more expensive. Add in the legal requirements for leases longer than 28 days, maintenance access challenges, and changing insurance implications, and you start to see why midterm rentals require just as much effort—if not more—than short-term stays. The takeaway? Midterm rentals can absolutely work, but only if you understand what you’re getting into. They aren’t easier—they’re just di

title: Hosting Hotline: Is One Week-Long Booking Better Than Multiple Short Stays?
description: How to set the right strategy for event-driven demand at your short-term rental. When a big event comes to town — whether it’s a music festival, sports championship, or annual parade — short-term rental hosts know it’s an opportunity to earn big. But the question many hosts face is this: should you push for one guest to book the entire week, or keep your calendar open for multiple shorter stays? In this Hosting Hotline episode, Sarah and Annette walk through exactly how to think about these situations, using a listener’s real-world example. Shaune hosts in a small town with a week-long festival, and he’s wondering if a single week-long booking is the smartest way to go. Here’s the breakdown of the three main strategies: 1. Require a Week-Long Stay If your market shows consistent demand for guests booking seven nights or more, this can simplify your hosting life. Fewer turnovers, fewer chances for guest issues, and a guaranteed booking locked in well ahead of time. But here’s the risk: if travelers only come for two or three nights of the event, you could sit empty because your minimum stay requirement doesn’t match demand. 2. Allow Shorter Stays at Premium Rates Sometimes the real revenue opportunity comes from stacking shorter stays. Two- and three-night bookings can be priced at a premium — and guests will pay it to secure those peak nights. Think about your own experiences: have you ever gladly paid above-market rates for a last-minute hotel room during a concert, sporting

title: How to Capture Event-Driven Demand with PriceLabs
description: Use local insights and dynamic pricing tools to maximize short-term rental revenue. When big events come to town, short-term rental demand doesn’t just rise—it explodes. Festivals, sporting events, conventions, and even smaller “sleeper” happenings can drive market compression, fill hotels, and leave travelers scrambling for alternative accommodations. That’s where smart hosts step in. In this week’s episode of Thanks for Visiting , Sarah and Annette break down how to spot and plan for demand spikes with the help of PriceLabs dynamic pricing software . By layering in your local knowledge—like knowing which teams travel well or which events draw passionate fans—you can set premium rates and actually book them. The duo walks through their annual and quarterly planning sessions, how to catch hidden opportunities, and why the mix of data and human insight is the real key to event-pricing success. If you’ve ever wondered how to stop leaving money on the table during high-demand weekends, this episode is your playbook. Download a transcript of this episode . title: Hosting Hotline: What Should You Do If Your New Listing Isn’t Getting Bookings?
description: Why the problem isn’t always the algorithm — and the steps you can take today to turn things around. Launching a short-term rental is exciting. You’ve poured time, energy, and money into getting your property guest-ready. But what happens when your listing goes live…and nothing? In this Hosting Hotline episode, Sarah and Annette answer a frustrated new host who hasn’t received a single booking in their first month. Their neighbor’s house (with a pool) looks busy, and they’re wondering if saturation, fees, or bad luck are the problem. Here’s the truth: every host will face stretches of low demand. Success comes from focusing on what you can control. Sarah and Annette outline a clear framework: 1. Audit Your Listing Presentation Professional photos, guest-focused descriptions, accurate amenities, and a frictionless booking process are non-negotiables. Sometimes hosts overlook simple issues like blocked calendars, confusing fees, or minimum stays that scare guests away. 2. Identify True Competition It’s easy to compare your property to the wrong neighbor. If your listing doesn’t have a pool, don’t benchmark yourself against one that does. Use dynamic pricing software to see exactly where you fall against properties that share your core features and guest avatar. 3. Leverage Your Data Airbnb’s Insights tab offers powerful information if you turn on professional hosting tools. You can see if guests are finding your property, how often they click through, and where conversions drop
