title: Stop Leaving Money on the Table: Mastering Far Out Premium Pricing
description: Getting booked first isn’t always a win—how to set far out premiums that protect your calendar and maximize revenue. If you’ve ever felt a rush of pride when your short-term rental got booked months (or even a year) in advance, you’re not alone. Many hosts see early bookings as a badge of honor—but the truth is, being “first booked” often means you left money on the table. Enter Far Out Premium Pricing . This strategy allows you to apply a markup to dates that extend beyond your typical booking window. Guests who crave certainty—like early planners or repeat visitors—are often willing to pay more for peace of mind. And by adding a premium to these far-out dates, you’re not penalizing them, you’re protecting your calendar and rewarding yourself for patience. Here’s what makes far out premiums so powerful: They capture peak revenue. Instead of guests locking in lower rates before demand spikes, you hold out for the true market value. They save you time. With tools like PriceLabs, once you set your rules, your rates automatically adjust every day. They shift your mindset. Stop chasing vanity metrics like a fully booked calendar and start focusing on profitability. They compound over time. Every booking builds on the last, teaching you more about your market and boosting your revenue. At the end of the day, premium pricing only works when paired with a premium guest experience. Deliver five-star stays, maintain strong operations, and then let your pricing strategy do the rest. Fa

title: Hosting Hotline: Should You Use a Company Logo on Airbnb?
description: Can a company logo build guest trust—or is your face still your best marketing tool? This week on the Hosting Hotline, Sarah is flying solo—but not alone. With Annette on vacation, she’s joined by Colleen Prochaska, COO of Thanks for Visiting , to answer a listener question that hits at the heart of hospitality: should you use your company logo as your Airbnb profile photo? Colleen and Sarah break down what Airbnb actually allows, what’s smart from a business perspective, and how your photo influences guest trust. While Airbnb doesn’t forbid using a company logo, the platform is designed to highlight personal connection. Guests often prefer knowing who they’re booking with—and a friendly, smiling photo can make all the difference. In fact, Harvard research shows that Airbnb hosts who smile in their photos receive about 3% more bookings than those who don’t. The pair also touch on why some hosts choose to hide behind a logo due to discrimination—a heartbreaking but important reality of hosting. Still, if your goal is to build long-term relationships and repeat guests, Colleen and Sarah agree: authenticity wins. Instead of trying to drive guests off Airbnb through your logo, focus on providing a five-star experience that makes them want to book directly next time. Hospitality starts with a smile—and sometimes, it really is that simple. Resources Mentioned: Airbnb Terms of Service — Review Airbnb’s official profile photo policies to ensure compliance. title: Inside a 10,000-Square-Foot STR: Lessons from Andrew Llewellyn
description: How one entrepreneur built a thriving large-group rental on the Kentucky Bourbon Trail—after 40 banks said no What happens when you blend ice cream, muffins, and entrepreneurship with a healthy dose of persistence? For Andrew Llewellyn, the result was a 10,000-square-foot short-term rental in Louisville that now welcomes milestone celebrations, bachelor parties, and corporate retreats. Andrew’s story didn’t start with real estate. He first ran ice cream shops and a food truck before pivoting during COVID. When plans for a bakery fell through, he turned to Airbnb—transforming a building into a one-of-a-kind large-group property that can host up to 42 guests. But success didn’t come easy. Andrew faced major challenges: Financing hurdles: 40 banks turned him down before one finally said yes. Renovation struggles: Managing construction while running another business pushed him to his limits. Operations at scale: Turning over a property for 42 guests required building a systemized cleaning team from the ground up. Through it all, Andrew learned to stay focused on the guests he wanted to serve. By targeting milestone events and corporate groups, he created a property that delivers exactly what his customers need—and commands premium rates. Today, Andrew balances parent-child listings to maximize occupancy, leans on dynamic pricing to stay competitive, and uses blogging and SEO to grow direct bookings. His advice to other hosts? Keep going. Persistence pays off, even when the obstac

title: Hosting Hotline: Are You Charging Enough? Pricing Strategies Every Host Should Know
description: Are You Leaving Money on the Table? Hosts often equate success with five-star reviews—but as Colleen says, “You can’t pay your mortgage with reviews.” In other words, high ratings are great, but they don’t always reflect the health of your business. To truly understand how your property is performing, you need to look at your numbers : revenue, occupancy rate, and how you stack up against comparable properties in your market. What “Doing Well” Really Means Before you celebrate your full calendar or great guest feedback, define your version of success. Do you want to increase your revenue by a certain percentage? Improve your average nightly rate? Reach a specific occupancy goal? Clarity around these targets is essential for making informed pricing decisions. How to Use Dynamic Pricing Tools Effectively Tools like PriceLabs, Beyond Pricing, and Wheelhouse are designed to help you stay competitive—but they’re not magic. Think of them as data partners, not decision-makers. The software gathers real-time market data, but it’s up to you to interpret that information and make adjustments based on your property, seasonality, and local demand. As Colleen explains, “PriceLabs doesn’t price your property—you do.” The tool provides insights, but you have to act on them consistently. Pricing Is Never Set It and Forget It The market is constantly changing—so should your pricing. Check in weekly to make small adjustments based on booking trends, events in your area, and seasonal shifts. Th

title: Why Per-Guest Fees Are Killing Your Conversion Rate
description: That “extra guest” fee might be doing more harm than good. Here’s how to fix it. It sounds logical—more guests mean more mess, right? But adding per-person fees might be the silent reason your short-term rental isn’t performing as well as it could. With Airbnb’s all-in pricing model, per-guest fees now get baked into the total nightly rate. Guests don’t see your breakdown—they just see a high price and keep scrolling. What feels like a smart way to cover costs could actually be driving guests away. Instead, Sarah and Annette share a more transparent (and profitable) solution: creating “parent” and “child” listings. The parent listing includes your full capacity and cleaning fee, while the child listing is optimized for smaller groups with a slightly lower rate. It’s simple, fair, and proven to improve conversion rates. Because pricing shouldn’t feel like a math test—it should feel easy, honest, and guest-friendly. Download a transcript of this episode. title: Hosting Hotline: What to Do When Airbnb Suspends Your Listing (and Won’t Tell You Why)
description: When one host’s new property was blocked for mysterious “violations,” Sarah and Annette shared exactly how to fight back—and protect your business from relying too much on one platform. Imagine buying a short-term rental, updating the design, and getting ready to launch—only to find your Airbnb listing suspended with no clear reason why. That’s what happened to Renee, a Superhost from Tucson, Arizona. In this Hosting Hotline episode, Sarah and Annette explain what likely happened behind the scenes (spoiler: it’s usually a trust and safety violation tied to the property’s address, not the host). They share actionable advice for hosts facing similar situations, including how to escalate your case, gather proof of compliance, and use public support channels to get Airbnb’s attention. But the real takeaway? You can’t build a sustainable business on someone else’s platform. Use moments like this as a catalyst to diversify—whether that means launching a direct booking website, expanding to VRBO or Booking.com, or targeting midterm renters. Because at the end of the day, Airbnb doesn’t own your property—you do. Resources Mentioned: Lodgify – All-in-one software to manage bookings, sync calendars, and build your direct booking site. Have More Questions? We’d love to hear from you! Submit your hosting questions to the Hosting Hotline and tune in to the podcast for answers. title: How to Copy Our Guest Marketing Emails and Actually Hit Send
description: Stop Letting Your Email List Collect Dust—Start Using It to Drive Bookings Most hosts focus all their energy on optimizing listings—but forget to nurture the guests who’ve already stayed. In this week’s episode, Sarah and Annette share five simple email ideas you can copy, tweak, and send right away. These strategies will help you build direct relationships with past guests, fill your calendar faster, and take ownership of your marketing. Why Email Still Matters Your email list is the one piece of marketing you truly own. When you rely solely on booking platforms, they control the guest relationship. Email lets you communicate on your own terms, share updates, and build brand loyalty long after check-out. Five Email Ideas You Can Start With: The Newsletter: A repeatable format that shares local updates, property news, or seasonal promotions. Guide-Style Tips: Curate local favorites—coffee shops, trails, restaurants, or events—to show off your expertise. Last-Minute Availability Blast: Turn open calendar days into revenue by reaching out to past guests first. New Amenity or Listing Alert: Whether it’s a new sauna or upgraded coffee maker, let guests know what’s new. Memorable Guest Experience: Share a favorite review, photo, or story that captures the feeling of staying with you. Bonus Idea: Send a short survey to learn what guests want more of—new amenities, seasonal experiences, or how often they’d like to hear from you. title: Hosting Hotline: Airbnb Promotions: Visibility Tool or Discount Trap?
description: Promotions can help your listing surface in search—but only if you use them strategically. Here’s how to make them work for you. When most hosts think of running a promotion on Airbnb, they think “discount.” But Sarah and Annette want you to reframe it as a visibility tool . Airbnb’s promotion system doesn’t pull directly from your nightly rate. Instead, it calculates the discount off your median rate from the past 60 days. That means even a 5–10% promo can be powerful if used at the right time. Promotions are most effective when: Demand is strong but you haven’t been booked yet Your typical booking window has passed You’re at risk of being buried in search results Used strategically, promotions signal to Airbnb’s algorithm that your listing deserves a boost. That can trigger higher visibility, special offer badges, and even inclusion in targeted guest emails. The key is moderation—track what works, remove promos when your dates fill, and don’t rely on discounts to drive demand. This is one lever in your business toolbox, not your safety net. Resources Mentioned: Airbnb Help Article Lodgify – All-in-one software to manage bookings, sync calendars, and build your direct booking site. Have More Questions? We’d love to hear from you! title: The 2025 Tax Rulebook for STR Hosts: What You Need to Know
description: CPA Amanda Han breaks down the new tax bill, what’s changing for hosts, and how to make smarter financial moves this year. Every host knows tax season can be daunting — but understanding your numbers is one of the most powerful ways to grow your business. In this episode of Thanks for Visiting , Sarah and Annette welcome back CPA and real estate tax strategist Amanda Han to unpack the One Big Beautiful Bill and what it means for short-term rental operators in 2025. Amanda explains the biggest updates to depreciation, deductions, and material participation — and why most STR owners should be paying far less in taxes than they think. She also shares practical advice on bookkeeping, finding the right CPA, and setting up financial systems that actually work. One reminder that stood out from Amanda during the conversation: “You can’t make decisions without numbers.” It’s a truth every host should live by. Whether you’re evaluating a new property, setting rates, or planning for tax season, your ability to grow depends on understanding what your numbers are actually telling you. Amanda’s insight reframes tax planning as a powerful business tool — not just a yearly obligation. With the right bookkeeping systems and strategy, hosts can uncover opportunities to save money, reinvest intelligently, and strengthen their financial foundation for years to come. If you’re serious about building long-term wealth through short-term rentals, this is a must-listen episode. Key Takeaways: Tax cha

title: Airbnb’s New Cancellation Rules: What Hosts Need to Do Now
description: How to stay profitable, prepared, and level-headed as Airbnb shifts to guest-first cancellation policies. Airbnb has officially rolled out its new 24-hour free cancellation policy for all stays under 28 days. And while this move brings Airbnb in line with hotels and airlines, it also introduces a new layer of uncertainty for hosts—especially those who rely on longer lead times or luxury bookings. If this update has you feeling frustrated or blindsided, you’re not alone. But with the right mindset and strategy, you can adapt quickly and keep your revenue strong. What Changed—and Why It Matters As of October 1, 2025, every guest who books at least seven days before check-in is entitled to a full refund within 24 hours of booking. New listings can no longer choose a “strict” cancellation policy, and existing listings may not retain that option forever. This is part of Airbnb’s broader push to streamline the guest experience and make cancellation terms more predictable across the platform. The Business Owner Mindset Shift Hosts who succeed long-term will be the ones who treat cancellations as a normal cost of doing business. Just like you plan for maintenance, damages, or chargebacks, you now need to plan for booking churn. The goal: stop being emotionally knocked off course when a cancellation comes in. How to Stay Ahead of These Changes Track your cancellation rate monthly so you can spot patterns. Build a financial cushion that assumes a small percentage of bookings will fall

title: Hosting Hotline: How to Pick the Right Guest Avatar for Your STR
description: How to decide which guest type will maximize your bookings—and your revenue. Choosing the right guest avatar is one of the most strategic decisions you'll make as a short-term rental host. And while many hosts want to appeal to everyone, the truth is simple: when you confuse, you lose. Guests need to immediately know who your space is designed for and what type of stay it delivers. This week on the Hosting Hotline, Emily from Australia brings a thoughtful challenge: her property is naturally aligned with families, but it’s also steps away from one of the region’s top wedding venues. Should she design her listing for the avatar she relates to—or the market that’s most profitable? Two Strong Avatars. Two Very Different Realities. A family-friendly stay aligns with Emily’s lifestyle and strengths as a host. These guests typically book earlier, stay longer, and travel with more needs and gear—but often at more budget-conscious price points. Wedding and event travelers, on the other hand, book closer to arrival and are often willing to pay premium rates for convenience. If your property sits near a major venue, this can dramatically boost your ADR and occupancy. Define What Success Means for You Sarah and Annette encourage hosts to clarify what matters most: If profitability is your top priority, the wedding guest market may be the strongest path. If you want deep alignment with the experience you’re creating, a family-centered stay may be the right choice—even if revenue is lower

title: Hosting Hotline: Should You Really Provide Shampoo in Every Bathroom?
description: Should You Really Provide Shampoo in Every Bathroom? The Answer Might Surprise You . Every host wants to elevate their guest experience—but when it comes to stocking shampoo, conditioner, and body wash, many hosts still hesitate. Is it worth the cost? Does it matter? Are you just copying hotels? In today’s Hosting Hotline episode, Sarah and Annette unpack why bathroom essentials are no longer a “nice touch”—they’re a baseline expectation in 2025. And failing to provide them can instantly downgrade an otherwise great stay. Drawing from their own recent Nashville experience and insights from Minoan, they explain why these simple amenities offer a high return in professionalism, clarity, and guest satisfaction. They also share how consistent bathroom standards streamline your operations and reinforce your brand in subtle but powerful ways. If you’re aiming to professionalize your short-term rental, this episode breaks down why bathroom basics aren’t optional—and how providing them positions you for better reviews, smoother operations, and repeat bookings. title: How to Shop Your Competition Using ChatGPT and Boost Bookings Fast
description: Turn Your Competitors’ Reviews Into Your Most Valuable Business Asset Most hosts know they should understand their competition, but very few know how to analyze their market in a meaningful way. Instead, they rely on intuition, casual browsing, or guesswork. The result? Missed opportunities and unclear positioning. In this episode, Sarah and Annette reveal a simple workflow that allows you to shop your competition the same way professional revenue managers do—using nothing more than ChatGPT and the publicly available reviews on competing listings. It all starts with building a strong comp set. Hosts often think their listing is so unique that they don’t have competitors, but OTAs tell a different story. When you search your destination on Airbnb or VRBO, the platform shows shoppers a curated group of comparable properties—and those listings define your competitive landscape. Once you identify three to five top performers in your area, the magic begins. By pulling 50–100 reviews from those listings and feeding them through a detailed ChatGPT prompt, you can uncover: • What guests consistently rave about • What frustrates them across multiple properties • Which amenities make a measurable impact on satisfaction • Which themes help shape an effective brand position The result is a clear, data-driven picture of your market—one you can use to refine your listing copy, prioritize upgrades, and differentiate your property with precision. This is the kind of analysis agencies charge

title: Hosting Hotline: How to Align With the Right Guests Without “Screening”
description: A practical, hospitality-first approach to evaluating bookings and setting expectations. When Alexandra asked how to “screen” guests, Sarah and Annette immediately reframed the question. In the short-term rental space, words carry weight—and screening can unintentionally lead hosts toward a mindset of judgment or assumption. Instead, the goal is simple: make sure your property is the right fit for a guest’s stay . Inside the episode, the hosts break down how to move from screening to alignment , including: How Airbnb’s verification and review system plays into trust Why Instant Book is often a revenue advantage What red flags actually matter when reviewing a booking The role of proactive communication in setting expectations The one question that consistently clarifies guest intent This episode reinforces a core TFV principle: most travelers have good intentions, and a consistent, hospitality-driven process is the best way to protect your property while still delivering a great guest experience. title: How to Boost Income in Slow Seasons: A Strategic Plan Every Host Needs
description: Your slow season isn’t a setback—it’s a tool for building a stronger, more profitable business. Most hosts try to “beat” their slow season. Sarah and Annette explain why that approach doesn’t work—and what to do instead. A slow season simply means this: market demand is lower overall. It’s not a reflection of your listing. When occupancy drops below 30% in your area, you’re in your slow season—period. Instead of chasing bookings that aren’t there, this episode outlines a smarter path: Refine your operations with deep cleaning, maintenance, and updated systems Review your financial foundation so you know exactly where money is working (and where it isn’t) Reconnect with past guests to understand who’s booking and why Invest in your own skills so you enter peak season with clarity and confidence The hosts also share how using this time intentionally gives you the space to improve your listing, strengthen your pricing strategy, and reduce overwhelm once peak season hits. Slow season isn’t about fixing anything—it’s about preparing for the moment when demand returns and making sure you’re ready to capture all of it. Download a transcript of this episode . title: Hosting Hotline: What to Do Before You Get the Keys to Your First STR
description: How to use the waiting period before closing to build a strong foundation for your hosting business. When Randa wrote in, she was doing what many new hosts do—thinking about furniture, design, and aesthetics while waiting to take possession of her first short-term rental. Sarah and Annette explain why this phase is actually the most important time to focus on building the business behind the property. Before a single guest checks in, there’s work that can’t wait: Setting up clean financial systems and business bank accounts Building a trusted advisory team for legal, insurance, and tax strategy Researching your market, seasonality, and revenue potential Learning how to price and manage your calendar strategically Creating operational habits that will support you once things get busy The hosts also stress the importance of avoiding information overload. While education matters, overconsuming advice can lead to hesitation instead of action. This episode reinforces a key truth: you don’t need the keys to start running your STR like a real business. title: The Real Risks of Using AI in Your Short-Term Rental Business
description: Where AI can support your hosting business—and where human expertise is still essential. AI tools like ChatGPT have made it easier than ever for hosts to write listings, automate guest messaging, and streamline operations. But as Sarah and Annette explain in this episode, not all uses of AI are created equal. Joined by attorney Scott Brown, the conversation dives into the legal and operational risks hosts often overlook—especially when AI is used to draft contracts, guest agreements, or management documents. Key takeaways include: Why AI-generated contracts can include incorrect or fabricated legal references How AI pulls information from broad, unvetted sources Why “looking legitimate” doesn’t mean something is legally sound Where AI is most effective as a learning or organizational tool How hosts should approach AI as an assistant—not a decision-maker The episode reinforces a critical mindset shift: AI can support your business, but it cannot replace trusted professional guidance. Used wisely, it can save time. Used carelessly, it can expose you to unnecessary risk. Download a transcript of this episode . title: Hosting Hotline: Choosing Your Lane—Revenue, Experience, or Both?
description: How clarifying your “why” helps you make confident, aligned decisions in your hosting business. Vanessa’s off-grid river cabin sparked a conversation that goes far beyond amenities. Her question wasn’t really about water—it was about identity. Do you build a short-term rental to maximize revenue? To support a specific lifestyle? Or to create a highly intentional, niche experience? Sarah and Annette explain that there’s no universal “right” answer, only the one that aligns with your goals. Adding water could expand demand. Staying off grid could create a deeply intentional guest experience. Both are valid if they’re chosen on purpose. Inside the episode, they break down how to: Identify what truly motivates your hosting decisions Recognize when data should inform—not dictate—your strategy Accept that narrowing your audience can be a strength Let go of the idea that every property needs to appeal to everyone Fully commit once you’ve made a values-based decision The core takeaway is simple but powerful: professional hosts don’t waffle—they decide, commit, and build accordingly. title: Hosting Hotline: Should You Adjust Airbnb Fees in PriceLabs or Airbnb?
description: How to handle Airbnb’s new host fees without distorting your base rate or pricing strategy Airbnb’s updated host fee structure has created confusion across the short-term rental industry, especially for hosts using dynamic pricing tools like PriceLabs. In this Hosting Hotline episode, Sarah and Annette respond to a question from Jenna Louise, who asked whether management fee adjustments should be made through PriceLabs or directly inside Airbnb. The short answer: those changes should not be made in your dynamic pricing software. Management fees can only be adjusted inside Airbnb or your property management system. PriceLabs does not currently support fee adjustments through its platform and more importantly, your base rate should remain your pricing compass. Distorting that number to recoup fees can create long-term pricing problems and unreliable data. The conversation also highlights why there is no universal percentage that will fully recapture Airbnb’s new host fee. Factors like average daily rate, length of stay, taxes, and additional fees all impact how the change shows up in your payouts. What works for one property may fall short, or overshoot, for another. Sarah and Annette emphasize the importance of monitoring every payout during this transition. Airbnb is still actively adjusting the rollout, and pricing platforms are responding in real time. This is not the moment for automation without understanding. Instead, hosts should focus on stability, data literacy, and i

title: Where to Splurge—and Where to Save—When Furnishing a Short-Term Rental
description: A practical, experience-backed guide to investing in the things guests actually care about—and skipping the rest. Furnishing a short-term rental isn’t the same as furnishing your own home—and treating it like one is one of the most expensive mistakes hosts make. We see hosts overspend on features guests barely notice while cutting corners on the things that directly impact comfort, reviews, and repeat bookings. The result? Wasted money, disappointing guest experiences, and properties that don’t perform the way they should. After furnishing dozens of short-term rentals and helping hosts across the country do the same, we’ve learned what actually matters—and what doesn’t. Here’s how to think strategically about where to splurge and where to save when furnishing your short-term rental. Where to Splurge 1. Beds and Bedding Your guests are booking an overnight stay—sleep matters. Cheap metal bed frames, low-quality mattresses, and flimsy linens lead to noise, discomfort, and negative reviews. A sturdy bed frame, a supportive mattress, and hospitality-grade sheets are some of the fastest paths to five-star reviews. Guests notice, talk about it, and remember it. 2. Cookware and Kitchen Essentials The kitchen is one of the biggest advantages short-term rentals have over hotels, yet it’s one of the most commonly under-equipped spaces. Warped pans, mismatched dishes, and not having enough place settings frustrate guests—especially families and longer stays. Quality cookware and ample d

title: Hosting Hotline: Does Renaming Airbnb Photos Actually Improve SEO?
description: How intentional photo naming supports search alignment, accessibility, and long-term listing performance. When hosts think about SEO, they usually focus on titles, descriptions, and amenities—but images play a role too. In this Hosting Hotline episode of Thanks for Visiting, Sarah and Annette answer a listener question about whether renaming Airbnb photos with keywords actually improves search visibility. The short answer? Yes—but not in the way most hosts think. SEO Is About Matching Search Behavior Airbnb has made it clear they’re focused on matching listings to guest intent . That means guests aren’t searching for hype-driven adjectives like “luxury” or “stunning.” They’re searching for concrete features: Walk to downtown King bed Hot tub Pet friendly Near convention center Renaming your images using these types of descriptors helps reinforce what your listing actually offers—especially when those terms reflect repeatable, predictable search patterns in your market. Why Image Names Matter By default, photos uploaded from cameras or phones are labeled with random identifiers. When those images are uploaded to Airbnb, VRBO, or your direct booking site, that data can still be read by platforms and search engines. Renaming an image from something like H58A1972.jpg to king-bedroom-blackout-curtains.jpg gives platforms clearer signals about the content of the image—and helps align your listing with relevant guest searches. Accessibility Is Part of the Equation Image naming isn’t

title: How AI Is Changing the Way Guests Find and Book Your Short Term Rental
description: How Do Guests Find Short Term Rentals Using AI? Guests are increasingly using AI tools to search for travel recommendations instead of traditional search engines. Rather than scrolling through pages of search results, travelers ask AI models direct questions about location, amenities, pet policies, and experiences. AI then pulls answers from websites and listings it understands and trusts. If your content does not clearly answer those questions, your property may not appear at all. What Is Generative Engine Optimization for Hosts? Generative engine optimization, often called GEO, is the process of structuring your content so AI models can easily understand and surface it in responses. Unlike traditional SEO, which focuses on keywords and backlinks, GEO prioritizes: Clear questions and direct answers Natural language that mirrors how people speak Content that explains context, not just features For hosts, this means your website should clearly explain: Where your property is located Who it is best for What makes it unique Why a guest would choose it over another option How Should Hosts Structure Their Website for AI Search? AI models look for clarity. Instead of vague marketing language, hosts should use question based headings and straightforward explanations. For example: Where is this property located? Is this rental pet friendly? What amenities does it offer? Who is this property ideal for? Each section should clearly answer the question in plain language. This makes it ea

title: Hosting Hotline: Why We’re Sunsetting the Hotline and What Comes Next
description: A behind-the-scenes look at why Thanks for Visiting is shifting focus and how hosts can apply “less but better” to their own businesses. For years, the Hosting Hotline was one of the most personal ways Sarah and Annette connected with hosts. Hearing your voices. Listening to real-time challenges. Helping you work through questions as they happened. It was meaningful, memorable, and incredibly special — for both sides of the microphone. But as Thanks for Visiting evolved, something became clear. The questions coming in were getting bigger. More nuanced. More tied to individual markets, financials, guest avatars, and long-term goals. And quick answers — without context — were no longer enough. Again and again, Sarah and Annette found themselves saying the same thing: “I wish I had more information.” “I need to see the listing.” “We need to understand your numbers.” Hosting isn’t a side hustle. It’s a business. And real businesses require depth, data, and strategy. Why the Hosting Hotline Had to Change The Hosting Hotline was created to connect, to listen, and to help hosts feel less alone. And it did exactly that. But over time, it became clear that the most meaningful transformations weren’t happening in quick call-ins. They were happening in spaces where hosts could share full context — dashboards, listings, challenges, and goals — and get strategic guidance in return. That’s where deeper change happens. Producing the Hosting Hotline took significant time and resources. And w

title: How To Plan Your 2026 Holiday Pricing Without Leaving Money on the Table
description: A strategic breakdown of the 2026 holiday calendar and how hosts can avoid blocked calendars, forced turnovers, and missed revenue. Holiday pricing is not about the holiday itself — it’s about how guests plan their entire trip around it. And in 2026, the calendar is working against hosts who aren’t paying attention. With major holidays falling on Fridays and Saturdays, guest arrival patterns, departure days, and minimum stay logic all change. If you default to the same holiday rules you’ve used in past years, you risk creating turnover chaos or leaving prime dates empty. Why the 2026 Calendar Matters More Than You Think Holidays aren’t isolated dates. They are booking windows. Guests don’t book “July 4th.” They book the experience around it — when they arrive, when they leave, how long they stay, and how smoothly the trip fits into their lives. When hosts ignore how holidays fall on the calendar, they either underprice premium windows or block themselves with rigid rules that don’t match modern travel behavior. Valentine’s Day and President’s Day: The First Test In 2026, Valentine’s Day falls on a Saturday and runs straight into President’s Day weekend. That combination matters — especially in ski markets, mountain towns, and drive-to destinations. You’ll see: Romantic weekend travelers booking shorter stays Families booking long weekends tied to school schedules Guests willing to pay a premium for certainty Early bookings that can block higher-value demand later If you don’t

title: Unconventional Ways Hosts Are Making Money Without Overnight Guests
description: How strategic hosts are unlocking new revenue streams by rethinking how their properties are used. Most hosts think of revenue in one way: overnight stays. But what if your property could generate income without anyone sleeping there? For strategic hosts, overnight bookings are just one piece of a bigger revenue picture — especially during shoulder seasons, slower days, or midweek gaps. In this episode of Thanks for Visiting , Sarah and Annette walk through unconventional ways hosts are making money by leveraging their properties differently, without sacrificing control or guest experience. Commercial Photography and Content Creation One of the highest-paying non-overnight uses hosts are seeing is commercial photography and content creation. Brands, photographers, and marketing teams constantly need beautiful, private spaces for: Brand shoots Product photography Website refreshes Social media campaigns These bookings often happen on weekdays, command higher hourly or daily rates, and create minimal wear compared to overnight guests. The key is having clear rules, usage agreements, and insurance awareness before saying yes. Day Use for Coworking and Strategy Days Short-term rentals are becoming an attractive alternative to coworking spaces and coffee shops. Entrepreneurs, consultants, and small teams are booking properties for: Planning days Strategy sessions Remote work meetups Why? Privacy, quiet, parking, kitchens, comfortable seating, and reliable Wi-Fi. Hosts who offer st

title: The Airbnb Metrics That Actually Drive Bookings (And the Mistake Most Hosts Make)
description: Stop guessing what’s wrong with your listing. Here's what Airbnb is already telling you. If you’ve ever said, “I feel like my listing is buried,” you’re not alone. But here’s the truth: feelings don’t get bookings — data does. Airbnb gives hosts access to powerful performance metrics inside its Professional Hosting Tools, yet most hosts either don’t know they exist or don’t know how to use them. As a result, they end up fixing the wrong things, overcorrecting, or making sweeping changes that actually hurt performance. In this episode of Thanks for Visiting , we break down the four Airbnb metrics that actually drive bookings and show you how to use them to make smarter, more strategic decisions. The Booking Funnel Most Hosts Ignore Every guest goes through a funnel before booking: They see your listing They click your listing They decide whether or not to book Each step has a metric tied to it. When you understand which step is underperforming, you know exactly what to fix — instead of guessing. The Four Airbnb Metrics That Matter 1. First Page Search Impression Rate This metric tells you how often Airbnb is showing your listing on page one. It’s about visibility, not desirability. Pricing, availability, minimum stay rules, and review ratings all influence whether Airbnb gives your listing a chance to be seen. 2. Search to Listing Conversion If guests see your listing but don’t click, the issue usually isn’t price — it’s your title, hero image, or value proposition. This is wh

title: How to Use a Virtual Assistant to Grow Your Hosting Business
description: Virtual assistants don’t replace you—they help you follow through. Many hosts say they’re overwhelmed because they have too much to do. But more often than not, the real problem isn’t workload — it’s follow-through. In this episode of Thanks for Visiting , we walk through how short-term rental hosts can use virtual assistants (VAs) to stay organized, execute consistently, and grow their business without becoming hands-off or disconnected. Virtual Assistants Are Not a Shortcut Let’s be clear: virtual assistants are not there to build your business for you. They are there to support the systems you put in place. When used correctly, a VA gives you leverage — not dependency. The key is intention. A VA relationship works best when you know what needs to be done, why it matters, and how success is measured. Five Ways Hosts Successfully Use Virtual Assistants 1. Daily, Weekly, and Monthly Task Management Start by identifying repetitive tasks that happen daily, weekly, or monthly. These are the first responsibilities to hand off. VAs help ensure nothing slips through the cracks while keeping priorities visible and consistent. 2. Guest Communication, Support, and Sales Virtual assistants can monitor inboxes, draft responses, flag priority messages, and support upsell opportunities like gap nights or add-ons. This frees hosts to focus on strategy instead of constant interruptions. 3. Operational Follow-Through Without Being On-Site From inventory tracking to vendor follow-ups, VAs hel

title: 2026 STR Predictions: What Hosts Need to Know Before the New Year
description: What hosts need to prepare for as the market continues to mature. As the short-term rental industry matures, hosts are entering a new era—one defined by higher standards, tighter regulations, and a market that rewards strategy over luck. After a year of normalization in 2025, Sarah and Annette are looking ahead to what 2026 will bring for hosts at every level. The Market Is Stabilizing—But Expectations Are Rising The days of rapid, post-pandemic growth are behind us. Occupancy in 2026 is expected to mirror 2025: stable, but competitive. Guests are comparing short-term rentals to hotels more than ever, expecting clarity, consistency, safety, and reliability at every touch point. Regulations Continue to Tighten Cities across the U.S. are enforcing and expanding the permitting frameworks they introduced in 2025. Compliance is no longer optional—it's your license to operate. Hosts who stay ahead of local rules will protect their business and the industry at large. Travel Patterns Are Continuing to Shift Drive-to markets and three- to four-night stays remain dominant as travelers prioritize convenience, flexibility, and affordability. Families and groups are breaking away from traditional seven-night bookings and choosing shorter, more frequent getaways. Trust Is the New Loyalty Travelers are mixing hotels and STRs within the same trip, meaning they’re loyal to quality, not platforms. Strong photos, transparent communication, and reliable reviews will make your listing stand out i

title: Annette + Sarah
description: Trailer | Thanks for Visiting! Hello, and welcome to Thanks for Visiting! We are your hosts Annette Grant and Sarah Karakaian, and we are here to encourage you to share your space and live your dreams! We discuss Patti’s fact-finding expeditions to Airbnbs in Europe, the long history between women and B&Bs, following the rules, furnishing apartments (with minimal IKEA), Patti’s worst Airbnb experiences, tips and tricks for managing multiple properties, and investing in yourself. title: What Hosts Can Learn From a World Traveler and Frequent Airbnb Guest

Hayden Lee is the host of Travel Stories Podcast, and he’s been traveling pretty much full-time since 2013. He recently became the first person to ever visit every country in mainland Europe on his motorcycle – during which he stayed in a lot of Airbnbs – and now he’s living in Brazil for a few months! We mine everything we can learn from his best and worst Airbnb experiences, and as an added bonus, he leaves us with an inspirational message that we know will inspire you to get up and take a step towards your dream! title: Our Interview with HGTV's Ashley Cordray and Michael Cordray
description: Restoring The Kettle House in Galveston & Hosting DIY/HGTV's Big Texas Fix – with Ashley Cordray & Michael Cordray (Episode 17) Ashley Cordray and Michael Cordray own a real estate renovation company that specializes in restoring homes from the late 19th and early 20th centuries. They are working with homes near the brink of destruction in their home town of Galveston, Texas, with hopes of restoring the island’s history and architectural styles. We recorded this episode from what is soon to be their first Airbnb, The Kettle House, which is a local landmark (it’s so famous that it’s a square in the local version of Monopoly). But here’s what makes the Cordrays two even cooler: their incredible story!! They met in 2010, at work, and then started dating in 2011. Two months later, they decided to move in together and purchase a fixer-upper in Galveston. In 2015, they were approached by a production company who knew they had the right stuff for a TV show – and Big Texas Fix is about to start airing its first season on the HGTV in June 2019 (you can already watch it on Amazon , though)! title: Turning a Shipping Container Into an Airbnb
description: The Box Hop: A Container Airbnb – with Emily Britt & Seth Britt (Episode 18) We’ve been spying on today’s guests on Instagram for a long time (@theboxhop – check it out), and we are so excited to finally be able to chat with them!! Emily and Seth Britt live in Columbus, and they love it, but they also yearn for a space where they can return to return to their rural roots. So Seth followed his long-time dream to build a shipping container home in one of their favorite places: the Hocking Hills of Ohio. It took one year of planning, three shipping containers, seven months of sweat and tears, a lot of help, and a whole lot of late nights, but they finally did it and it is BEAUTIFUL! Their desire is to now share this home and dream with others – and we are so excited to share them with you! title: Bonus. Jeff Iloulian
description: Bringing Buying Group Power To All STR Hosts Jeff Iloulian has been in the vacation rental industry for nearly a decade. He has set up, managed, and/or operated over 250+ vacation rental properties; from cozy cabins in the woods, to luxury mansions in Beverly Hills. Additionally, Jeff has successfully grown several businesses and has a passion for interdisciplinary problem solving in emerging industries. He firmly believes that the further you push outside of your comfort zone, the larger that zone becomes. With a background in Economics and Philosophy from Columbia University and a JD from UCLA School of law, Jeff spent three years as a lawyer practicing Business Litigation, Intellectual Property and Cybersecurity before becoming an entrepreneur. Jeff is now CEO of HostGPO, THE buying group for hosts, which helps all STR business owners get access to the industry’s best discounts on furniture, linens, and everything you need to create long-term growth for your short-term rental. title: Be Unreasonably Hospitable w/ Charlie Eich (VV4)
description: The Core of Any Successful Service–Based Business is Culture Welcome back to Success Unlocked, where our hosting business master membership coaches discuss operations, social media, unique builds, co-hosting strategies, and so much more. title: Hosting Hotline: Does My Indoor Security System Camera Break Airbnb Rules?
description: Airbnb Summer Update: No Interior Cameras Means No Interior Cameras (Episode 364) Welcome back to the Hosting Hotline! This is an Ask Me Anything where each week we’ll answer your questions on Airbnb, STRs, real estate, and everything in between. Becky called into our Hosting Hotline and asked: I have an ADT security system and my control panel has a little camera on it. It takes a picture of the person who is setting or disabling the alarm. Nobody ever sets the alarm in the Airbnb, but it is in the hallway. I was wondering… Does that count as a camera for Airbnb? If I put a sticker over it, would that work as not having a camera? This is such an important question to discuss. As hosts, we are always looking out for our guests and our properties. While the security system's intent is to protect the home and the people inside, we believe this is too much of a gray area. Airbnb's terms and conditions are very clear about the use of cameras inside properties. According to Airbnb, hosts must disclose all surveillance devices in their listings and must never put any surveillance devices inside properties. To be 100% compliant with Airbnb's terms and conditions, if your interior security panel has a camera, it is best to replace it with a different system without a camera. This ensures you respect guests' privacy while still maintaining the security of your property. By adhering to these guidelines, you avoid potential disputes or negative reviews and create a welcoming and trustwo

Customer Support Importance: Prioritize excellent customer support in your PMS choice, as this can greatly affect the efficiency of handling operational issues that arise at inconvenient times. Compare and Contrast: Make a pros and cons list for each PMS option and involve feedback from other users through forums or networks to understand the practical benefits and drawbacks. title: Airbnb’s New Policy Update: What Hosts Need to Know About Off-Platform Rules and Fee Transparency (Bonus)
description: On May 10, 2025, Airbnb’s updated Off-Platform and Fee Transparency Policy goes into effect—and if you’re a short-term rental host, there are important changes to understand. While some of the updates may seem like clarifications of existing rules, they come with new levels of enforcement and implications for how you operate your Airbnb listing. In this post, we’re breaking down what’s new, what it means for your business, and what actions you should take today to stay compliant (and even find new opportunities for revenue). What Is Airbnb’s New Policy All About? Airbnb is tightening its rules on: Off-platform communication and payments Mandatory fees and how they’re disclosed Approved software and API partners Security deposits and damage waivers If you’ve ever asked a guest to Venmo you for pool heat or collected a pet fee outside of the platform, this update is for you. No More Off-Platform Payments (Even for Upsells) One of the biggest changes: you can no longer collect payment outside of Airbnb for anything related to the reservation—unless you're using an Airbnb API-approved software partner . That includes: Pet fees Pool heat Early check-in or late checkout Any other upgrades or add-ons If it’s a mandatory fee , it must be included at the time of booking using Airbnb’s “additional fees” section. If it’s an optional upsell , it must be: Clearly listed in your Airbnb listing description Collected through the Airbnb Resolution Center (or via an approved software) Software
